target.title: build(pnpm): bump pnpm to 11.2.2 & use packageManager as pnpm source
target.body:
## Summary

Bumps pnpm to `11.2.2` and makes CI/release workflows use the repo's `packageManager` pin instead of separate hardcoded pnpm versions.

## Changed

- Updated the root `packageManager` pin to pnpm `11.2.2`.
- Removed workflow-level `PNPM_VERSION` constants and `pnpm-version` plumbing from CI/release workflows.
- Updated the shared pnpm setup action to resolve pnpm from `package.json#packageManager` by default, with an explicit override still available.
- Added workflow tests to keep pnpm version selection centralized.

## Risks

The branch is blocked until current lockfile entries age past the repo's `minimumReleaseAge: 2880` policy. Newer pnpm validates existing lockfile entries too, and several dependency updates currently in `pnpm-lock.yaml` are still less than 48 hours old.

References:
- https://github.com/orgs/pnpm/discussions/11377
- https://pnpm.io/settings#minimumreleaseage
- https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/releases/tag/v11.1.3

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `actionlint` on touched workflow files
- Cloudflare Crabbox focused workflow tests passed with `--config.minimum-release-age=0` after confirming the default install failure is the known release-age policy gate.

## Complexity

Reduced: pnpm version selection now has one source of truth in `package.json#packageManager`; workflows no longer carry separate pnpm pins.

target.title: fix(daemon): keep Linux minimal service PATH truly minimal
target.body:
## Summary
- stop treating Linux version-manager directories as part of the daemon's minimal service PATH
- keep stable user bin directories like ~/.local/bin, ~/.npm-global/bin, ~/bin, Nix profiles, and pnpm
- update Linux PATH tests to lock in the hardened-PATH behavior from #71578

## Testing
- pnpm test -- --run src/daemon/service-env.test.ts src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts
- pnpm check:docs

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Linux minimal service PATH generation should stop carrying version-manager-specific directories such as nvm/fnm/Volta/asdf/Bun while still keeping stable user-bin roots and canonical system directories.
- **Real environment tested:** Real OpenClaw source checkout on my local macOS machine (`/private/tmp/openclaw-pr71619`) using the patched repository and a live `pnpm exec tsx` invocation against `src/daemon/service-env.ts`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  pnpm exec tsx <<'TS'
  import { buildMinimalServicePath, getMinimalServicePathParts } from './src/daemon/service-env.ts';

  const existsSync = () => true;
  const withHome = getMinimalServicePathParts({
    platform: 'linux',
    env: {
      HOME: '/home/alice',
      NVM_DIR: '/home/alice/.nvm',
      FNM_DIR: '/home/alice/.local/share/fnm',
      VOLTA_HOME: '/home/alice/.volta',
      ASDF_DATA_DIR: '/home/alice/.asdf',
      BUN_INSTALL: '/home/alice/.bun',
      PNPM_HOME: '/home/alice/.local/share/pnpm',
      NPM_CONFIG_PREFIX: '/home/alice/.npm-global',
    },
    home: '/home/alice',
    existsSync,
  });
  const withoutHome = getMinimalServicePathParts({
    platform: 'linux',
    env: {},
    home: undefined,
    existsSync,
  });
  console.log('WITH_HOME=' + JSON.stringify(withHome));
  console.log('WITHOUT_HOME=' + JSON.stringify(withoutHome));
  console.log('BUILT=' + buildMinimalServicePath({
    platform: 'linux',
    env: {
      HOME: '/home/alice',
      NVM_DIR: '/home/alice/.nvm',
      FNM_DIR: '/home/alice/.local/share/fnm',
      VOLTA_HOME: '/home/alice/.volta',
      ASDF_DATA_DIR: '/home/alice/.asdf',
      BUN_INSTALL: '/home/alice/.bun',
      PNPM_HOME: '/home/alice/.local/share/pnpm',
      NPM_CONFIG_PREFIX: '/home/alice/.npm-global',
    },
    existsSync,
  }));
  TS
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:**
  ```text
  WITH_HOME=["/home/alice/.local/share/pnpm","/home/alice/.npm-global/bin","/home/alice/.local/bin","/home/alice/bin","/home/alice/.nix-profile/bin","/usr/local/bin","/usr/bin","/bin"]
  WITHOUT_HOME=["/usr/local/bin","/usr/bin","/bin"]
  BUILT=/home/alice/.local/share/pnpm:/home/alice/.npm-global/bin:/home/alice/.local/bin:/home/alice/bin:/home/alice/.nix-profile/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The live output from the patched checkout keeps stable user-bin and canonical system directories, while omitting `NVM_DIR`, `FNM_DIR`, `VOLTA_HOME`, `ASDF_DATA_DIR`, and `BUN_INSTALL` roots from the Linux minimal service PATH.
- **What was not tested:** I did not validate an actual Linux systemd-installed gateway on this machine; this proof is a live patched-checkout invocation of the production PATH-building code.

Closes #71578

commands.author: keshavbotagent

target.title: Preserve Codex and Telegram context evidence
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram prompt context cached inbound messages but not OpenClaw's own outbound Telegram replies, so a later context window could include Keshav's 01:37 request while omitting the adjacent 01:39 assistant answer.
- Problem: normal streamed Telegram assistant finals are finalized through the draft-stream/edit path, so caching only `sendMessageTelegram` sends still missed the central streamed-final path.
- Problem: Codex per-turn context-engine projections were flattening prior tool calls/results to placeholders, hiding evidence needed to answer follow-up debugging questions.
- Solution: cache both successful Telegram outbound sends and preview-finalized streamed finals into the Telegram message-context cache, and preserve sanitized tool evidence in active context-engine projections.
- Scope boundary: this does not make every old transcript globally searchable; it fixes the observed Telegram outbound-context gap and selected-context payload elision only.

## Motivation

Keshav pointed out that a later Codex turn did not show the earlier timeout-halving work or the 01:39 final answer evidence. Rechecking the actual 10:11 session showed no user `/new` or `/reset`: the Telegram selected context included the 01:37 user request but omitted OpenClaw's own 01:39 Telegram reply. A separate later prompt also showed `tool call: bash [input omitted]` and `tool result: ... [content omitted]`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #85219
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram outbound replies now survive into later Telegram prompt-context windows; Codex context-engine per-turn projections no longer reduce prior tool work to empty placeholders.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on this host, branch `fix/codex-context-tool-payloads`, head commit `f90ce9b590f4c08fb3f9f239735a767551a1f048`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran targeted Vitest coverage from the repo root. Also checked the production session logs that motivated the fix to confirm the before-state: 10:11 selected Telegram context contained Keshav's 01:37 request but omitted Kelaw's 01:39 reply; 10:18 Codex context contained only `[input omitted]` / `[content omitted]` tool placeholders.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied local output from the patched checkout:

```text
Test Files 4 passed (4)
Tests 218 passed (218)
```

- Observed result after fix: The Telegram send regression records a bot reply in a forum topic, then the next selected Telegram conversation context contains that bot reply body. The streamed-final regression records a preview-finalized assistant reply from the draft-stream path, then a later Telegram context window selects that assistant reply body. The projected Codex prompt includes useful result text like `already halved timeoutSeconds=7200`, includes tool input shape metadata, does not include `[content omitted]`, and does not expose raw shell command or secret-like token.
- What was not tested: A full live Telegram-to-Codex production turn was not exercised locally. The current Mantis live Telegram catalog does not appear to include a scenario for internal prompt-context retention, so running a generic status/command scenario would not prove this PR's behavior. A stronger live proof would require a dedicated QA scenario or a candidate deployment probe.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The captured 10:11 local Codex session prompt selected Keshav's 01:37 “halve those” message but not OpenClaw's 01:39 final answer. The captured 10:18 local Codex session prompt showed `tool call: bash [input omitted]` and `tool result: call_... [content omitted]`.

- Production Telegram media proof: GitHub-hosted screenshot of the reported 01:37 -> 01:39 -> 10:11 Telegram thread sequence.

  ![Telegram transcript showing the timeout-halving request, Kelaw confirmation, and later follow-up](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/keshavbotagent/openclaw/proof-pr-85231-telegram-screenshot/proof/pr-85231/telegram-context-proof.jpg)

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Telegram only recorded inbound messages in `message-cache.ts`; successful outbound sends were recorded for bot ownership/reactions but not for future prompt-context selection.
- Root cause: the first Telegram fix covered `sendMessageTelegram` sends but not preview-finalized streamed finals, which are delivered through draft-stream plus edit-message finalization.
- Root cause: `run-attempt.ts` passed `toolPayloadMode: contextEngineProjection ? "preserve" : "elide"`, so active per-turn context-engine projections used elision even when the projection engine had safe redaction support.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No regression covered Telegram outbound replies being present in later selected conversation context, streamed finals being written to the same cache, or active per-turn context-engine projections preserving redacted tool-result evidence.

## Prompt Boundary Review

ClawSweeper flagged the prompt-boundary expansion in `run-attempt.ts`. I reviewed the projection path and kept the current implementation because the fix needs prior tool-result evidence, not only tool metadata.

Boundary details:

- Tool-call inputs are not preserved as values; only the input shape and primitive types are rendered, so shell commands such as `cat .env` are omitted.
- Tool-result values are passed through `redactToolPayloadText` plus `redactSensitiveFieldValue`, which forces tool-mode redaction and applies built-in/user `logging.redactPatterns`.
- Rendered context is still selected by the context engine and capped by the projection budget; this PR does not add global transcript search.
- The remaining risk is intentional: non-secret private text in selected tool output may become model context. Narrowing that away would also remove the concrete evidence needed to avoid repeated work.

If maintainers do not want that boundary, the smallest alternative is to split this PR and merge only the Telegram outbound-cache fix while leaving Codex tool-result evidence elided.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts`, `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`, `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: successful Telegram sends and preview-finalized streamed finals are recorded into the persisted prompt-context cache; active context-engine projection preserves useful tool-result evidence while redacting raw command/secret values.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it covers both Telegram delivery paths that can produce visible assistant replies and the Codex prompt sent to `turn/start`.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `context-engine-projection.test.ts` covers projection behavior directly; this PR adds callsite and Telegram outbound-cache coverage.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Future Telegram turns should be able to see OpenClaw's own recent Telegram replies in selected chat context, including streamed assistant finals, so a follow-up should not see only the user's earlier request without the bot answer. Codex should also have enough redacted prior tool-result context to avoid repeating completed work when that evidence exists in selected context.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/message-cache.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`

target.title: Speed up /models browse replies
target.body:
## Summary

- use the read-only model catalog for default `/models` browse replies
- disable runtime auth discovery for that static browse visibility pass, matching the fast Gateway-style behavior
- keep explicit `/models ... all` on the full catalog path

Fixes #84731.

## Local verification

In a LiteLLM-backed environment with about 410 configured models, runtime patch testing reduced the auto-reply command timings from roughly:

- `/models`: ~69s -> ~1.3s
- `/models litellm`: ~28-32s -> ~1.3s
- `/models list litellm`: ~32s -> ~1.3s

Explicit `all` remains on the full path and stayed slow by design.

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.test.ts`

target.title: fix(cli): recover replaced device approvals
target.body:
## Summary

- Recover `openclaw devices approve <requestId>` when a local same-device repair request supersedes the request being approved.
- Require the replacement to match the original device identity, role/client metadata, and a compatible scope superset before approving it via local fallback.
- Add focused infra and CLI tests for devices-first requestId churn, cron-first progressive authority, replacement approval, fail-closed cases, and JSON/interactive output.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/cli/devices-cli.test.ts src/infra/device-pairing-churn.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw devices approve <requestId>` no longer fails with an unclear `unknown requestId` when the same local CLI repair request has been superseded by a compatible replacement during the approval reconnect.

Real environment tested: Local source checkout on Linux after rebasing onto current `origin/main`; focused Vitest shards for CLI and infra pairing behavior.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm test src/cli/devices-cli.test.ts src/infra/device-pairing-churn.test.ts`

Evidence after fix: Test output reported `src/infra/device-pairing-churn.test.ts (2 tests)` passed and `src/cli/devices-cli.test.ts (43 tests)` passed; `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD` produced no output.

Observed result after fix: same-device compatible replacement approvals use the replacement request ID, emit resolved metadata in JSON mode, print an interactive replacement notice, and fail closed for missing snapshots, incompatible scopes, cross-device replacements, public-key changes, role changes, metadata conflicts, remote `--url`, or missing pending requests.

What was not tested: Full packaged Docker/Crabbox smoke for the final branch; previous black-box validation was used to define the focused source-level regression coverage.

Refs: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/74484

target.title: fix(auth): cooldown inline api key billing failures
target.body:
## Summary
- track provider-level usage/backoff state for inline `models.providers.<id>.apiKey` credentials without creating synthetic auth profiles
- skip temporarily disabled inline API keys during auth resolution and availability checks
- mark inline API keys on provider auth/billing failures so repeated 402s do not immediately retry the same exhausted key

Closes #39807.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior addressed**: Inline `models.providers.<id>.apiKey` credentials now record provider auth/billing failures in usage state, stop immediate reuse during cooldown, and become available again after the cooldown expires without creating a synthetic auth profile.
- **Environment tested**: Local Linux workstation on PR branch `fix/inline-api-key-billing-cooldown` at `61ac6e93eac913fee891d3d63262518e08185c9d`, using Node/tsx against the production auth profile store, usage helpers, and model auth resolution helpers.
- **Steps run after the patch**: Ran a Node terminal probe that created a temporary agent directory, configured a dummy inline provider API key, resolved availability before failure, called `markInlineProviderApiKeyFailure(...)` with reason `billing`, checked availability/resolution during cooldown, then simulated cooldown expiry and resolved the inline key again.
- **Evidence**:

```text
[agent/embedded] auth profile failure state updated: runId=real-proof-inline-billing profile=sha256:f68577404b84 provider=demo-local reason=billing window=disabled reused=false
{
  "behavior": "inline provider apiKey billing failure disables only that inline key and blocks immediate reuse",
  "beforeFailure": {
    "hasAvailableAuth": true,
    "resolvedMode": "api-key",
    "resolvedSource": "models.json"
  },
  "recordedFailure": {
    "usageId": "inline-api-key:demo-local",
    "profileCreated": false,
    "disabledReason": "billing",
    "disabledUntilIsFuture": true
  },
  "duringCooldown": {
    "hasAvailableAuth": false,
    "blocked": true,
    "blockedMessage": "Inline API key for provider \"demo-local\" is temporarily disabled after a provider auth/billing failure. Retry after about 300 minutes, or switch to a different auth profile/API key."
  },
  "afterCooldownExpiry": {
    "hasAvailableAuth": true,
    "resolvedMode": "api-key",
    "resolvedSource": "models.json"
  }
}
```

- **Observed result**: Before the billing failure, the inline key is available and resolves from `models.json`. After `markInlineProviderApiKeyFailure(...)`, `usageStats["inline-api-key:demo-local"]` records `disabledReason: "billing"`, `disabledUntil` is in the future, no synthetic profile is created, `hasAvailableAuthForProvider(...)` returns false, and `resolveApiKeyForProvider(...)` blocks immediate reuse with the temporary-disabled error. After simulated cooldown expiry, the same inline key becomes available and resolves from `models.json` again.
- **Not tested**: A live paid provider 402 against a real provider account; this avoids intentionally causing a real billing failure while still exercising the production failure-marking and auth-resolution path with a redacted dummy inline key.

## Validation
- `pnpm test src/agents/auth-profiles.markauthprofilefailure.test.ts src/agents/model-auth.profiles.test.ts` (2 files, 66 tests passed)
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.ts src/agents/auth-profiles.ts src/agents/model-auth.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts src/agents/auth-profiles.markauthprofilefailure.test.ts src/agents/model-auth.profiles.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Additional real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed after review**: the actual embedded-runner failure paths now call the inline provider API-key marker even when the active credential has no profile id.
- **Environment tested**: local checkout after merging upstream/main into fix/inline-api-key-billing-cooldown at ce378baab4.
- **Evidence after fix**: added runner-path coverage that simulates an inline models.providers.openai.apiKey billing failure with no auth profile and verifies auth-state.json records inline-api-key:openai with disabledReason: "billing" and a future disabledUntil.
- **Validation after fix**:
  - ./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.ts src/agents/auth-profiles.ts src/agents/model-auth.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/assistant-failover.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/assistant-failover.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner.run-embedded-pi-agent.auth-profile-rotation.e2e.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles.markauthprofilefailure.test.ts src/agents/model-auth.profiles.test.ts

target.title: fix(feishu): prevent duplicate text send when sendMedia has no media URL
target.body:
## Summary

- In the Feishu `sendMedia` handler, text is sent unconditionally at line 160 before checking for `mediaUrl`
- When `sendMedia` is called with text but no media URL, the text-only fallback at line 196 sends the same text again
- This results in duplicate messages in Feishu chats

## Root cause

The `sendMedia` function has three code paths:

1. **Text + media**: sends text (line 161), then sends media (line 173) — correct
2. **Media only**: skips text (line 160), sends media (line 173) — correct
3. **Text only (no media)**: sends text (line 161), skips media (line 171), sends text AGAIN (line 196) — **bug**

The pre-media text send at line 160 was not guarded by a `mediaUrl` check, so it fires even when there is no media to follow.

## Fix

Add `&& mediaUrl` to the condition at line 160, so the companion text message is only sent when media follows it. The text-only fallback at line 196 handles the no-media case correctly on its own.

## Test plan

- [x] All 13 Feishu outbound tests pass (`pnpm test -- extensions/feishu/src/outbound`)
- [x] Code inspection confirms the three code paths now produce exactly one text send each

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(docker): add optional uv installation for skills and extensions
target.body:
## Summary
Add `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_UV` build arg to the Dockerfile to optionally install [uv](https://astral.sh/uv), a fast Python package manager, during image build.
## Motivation
Several OpenClaw workspace skills (e.g. `stock-analysis`) require uv for managing Python dependencies. In Docker deployments, manually installing uv inside a running container causes it to be lost on restart. This change allows uv to be baked into the image at build time, making it persistent across container restarts.
## Implementation
- New `ARG OPENCLAW_INSTALL_UV=""` build flag (default: off)
- When enabled, installs uv to `/usr/local/bin` so it is accessible to the non-root `node` user
- Uses the officialAstral install script: `curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh`
## Build example
```bash
docker build --build-arg OPENCLAW_INSTALL_UV=1 -t openclaw:uv .

target.title: fix(telegram): surface sticker file_id in inbound context
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram ingress already captures `TemplateContext.Sticker.fileId`, but agent-visible inbound prompt context did not surface a replayable sticker handle.
- Why it matters: native sticker turns could reach the agent without the exact `file_id` needed to send the same sticker back, which blocked sticker echo and curated native-sticker reply workflows.
- What changed: add `sticker_file_id` to per-turn `Conversation info (untrusted metadata)` in `buildInboundUserContextPrefix()`, keep it out of stable inbound system metadata, and lock the behavior with focused tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does **not** add sticker media passthrough/downloads, animated/video sticker handling, or broader sticker-ingress redesign.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature

- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #8758
- Related #21984
- Related PR #19056
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression


## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: `ctx.Sticker.fileId` was already populated by Telegram ingress, but inbound prompt construction did not project a replayable sticker identifier into the agent-visible per-turn context.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no focused test asserting the intended split between stable system metadata (must exclude per-turn sticker data) and per-turn conversation info (must include `sticker_file_id` for eligible chats).
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): prior PR #19056 proposed exposing sticker metadata in the stable system prompt. This branch intentionally takes the narrower safer route: conversation info only. Related product context also exists in #8758 and #21984.
- Why this regressed now: not a new regression; it is a long-standing Telegram prompt-context gap that became more obvious once native sticker send/reuse workflows were in play.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: ruled out Telegram Bot API limitations and provider-ingress absence; the missing piece was prompt projection, not sticker capture.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: direct Telegram chats include `sticker_file_id`; direct webchat sticker-only turns still emit no conversation metadata; stable system metadata never includes `sticker_file_id`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in prompt-shaping logic and can be verified deterministically without network IO or live Telegram.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none before this branch.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Telegram sticker turns can now expose a replayable `sticker_file_id` to the agent in per-turn conversation info, enabling native sticker reply workflows that depend on Telegram sticker file IDs.
- Stable inbound system metadata remains unchanged; `sticker_file_id` is intentionally excluded there.
- Direct webchat turns still omit conversation metadata, including sticker-only turns.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This adds one existing Telegram per-turn identifier (`sticker_file_id`) to the untrusted conversation-info block only when conversation info is already included. It remains excluded from stable system metadata and direct webchat sessions, which limits prompt-prefix churn and avoids broadening trusted metadata.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu (local dev host)
- Runtime/container: local Node/pnpm repo checkout
- Model/provider: N/A (prompt-shaping path)
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram
- Relevant config (redacted): standard Telegram bot ingress; no sticker-specific config required

### Steps

1. Build inbound context for a Telegram message with `TemplateContext.Sticker.fileId` set.
2. Inspect `buildInboundMetaSystemPrompt()` and `buildInboundUserContextPrefix()` output, or run `pnpm exec vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.test.ts`.
3. Confirm `sticker_file_id` appears only in per-turn conversation info for eligible chats and not in the stable system metadata block.

### Expected

- `sticker_file_id` is available to the agent in per-turn conversation info for Telegram chats where conversation info is included.
- `sticker_file_id` is omitted from stable inbound system metadata.
- Direct webchat sticker-only turns still emit no conversation metadata.

### Actual

- Before this branch: `sticker_file_id` was not surfaced to the agent at all.
- On this branch: the field is present in per-turn conversation info, excluded from stable system metadata, and covered by focused regression tests.

target.title: fix: serialize concurrent appendMessage calls to prevent synthetic flush race
target.body:
# fix: serialize concurrent appendMessage calls to prevent synthetic flush race

## Problem

OpenClaw's `session-tool-result-guard` incorrectly generates **synthetic error tool results** during normal agent operation when multiple async event handlers dispatch `appendMessage` concurrently. Users experience this as:

- Spurious `[tool call failed — synthetic result]` entries in session transcripts
- Duplicate or corrupted conversation histories (real result appended after synthetic)
- Agent loops that halt unexpectedly because the guard believes tool calls went unanswered

Affects any scenario where the event bus fires multiple listeners that each call `appendMessage` without awaiting the previous call — common during agent completion and parallel tool dispatch.

Closes #38432, #13351, #15008.

---

## Root Cause

Two interacting bugs in `session-tool-result-guard.ts`:

### Bug 1: No serialization of concurrent `guardedAppend` callers

The guard maintains a shared `Map<string, string>` called `pending` (tool call ID → tool name). The flush conditions that synthesize error results check `pending.size > 0` and fire on any non-`toolResult` message arriving while IDs are registered:

```
Tick 1:  Handler A → guardedAppend(assistantMsg with tool_calls)
           → pending.set("call_1", "read_file")
           → originalAppend(assistantMsg)  ← async, yields to event loop

Tick 2:  Handler B → guardedAppend(assistantMsg completion, no tools)
           → pending.size > 0 → flushPendingToolResults() fires 🔥
           → synthetic error written for "call_1"
           → pending.clear()

Tick 3:  Handler A resumes / real toolResult arrives
           → pending.delete("call_1")  ← no-op, already cleared
           → originalAppend(real toolResult) appended AFTER synthetic
           → Transcript: [tool_calls, synthetic_error, real_result] ❌
```

Because `guardedAppend` is `async`-capable (it returns whatever `originalAppend` returns, which may be a Promise), any awaited I/O inside `originalAppend` creates a window where a second concurrent caller observes `pending.size > 0` and flushes prematurely.

### Bug 2: `pending.set()` called after `originalAppend()`

Tool call IDs were registered in `pending` **after** `originalAppend(message)` was called. If `originalAppend` is async and yields, a concurrent `toolResult` for the same ID could arrive during that window, find the ID absent from `pending`, skip the delete, and leave the ID permanently registered — triggering a spurious synthetic on the next non-`toolResult` message.

---

## Fix

**2 files changed, +167 / −25 lines.**

### 1. Cooperative async mutex (`session-tool-result-guard.ts`)

A minimal promise-chain mutex (`createMutex()`) wraps the entire body of `guardedAppend`. Each call acquires the lock before executing and releases it on completion (including on error), so concurrent callers execute strictly sequentially in arrival order:

```typescript
function createMutex() {
  let queue: Promise<void> = Promise.resolve();
  return {
    run<T>(fn: () => T | Promise<T>): Promise<T> {
      const result = queue.then(() => fn());
      queue = result.then(() => undefined, () => undefined);
      return result;
    },
  };
}
```

The guard body is split into `guardedAppendInner` (logic) and `guardedAppend` (mutex wrapper), keeping the public installation API unchanged.

### 2. Reorder `pending.set()` to before `originalAppend()`

Tool call IDs are now registered in `pending` **before** `originalAppend(message)` is called, eliminating the window where a concurrent `toolResult` could arrive and find a missing ID:

```typescript
// Register BEFORE appending so a racing toolResult handler finds the IDs
if (toolCalls.length > 0) {
  for (const call of toolCalls) {
    pending.set(call.id, call.name);
  }
}
const result = originalAppend(message as never);
```

No new npm dependencies. No changes to `session-tool-result-guard-wrapper.ts`.

---

## Testing

**9/9 tests passing** (6 existing + 3 new).

### New concurrent tests added to `session-tool-result-guard.test.ts`:

| Test | What it covers |
|---|---|
| `does not synthesize when toolResult arrives concurrently with next assistant message` | Exact Tick 1/2/3 race scenario — fires `toolResult` and a completion message as concurrent microtasks; asserts no synthetic is written and real result is preserved |
| `serializes multiple concurrent appends without duplicating messages` | Two tool results dispatched concurrently for a two-tool assistant message; asserts exactly 3 messages with no synthetics |
| `getPendingIds() returns empty after concurrent resolution` | Confirms `pending` Map is fully drained after concurrent resolution (no leaked IDs that would trigger future spurious flushes) |

All three new tests **fail on the unpatched code** and pass on the patched code, confirming they directly exercise the bug.

---

## Breaking Changes

`guardedAppend` now always returns `Promise<ReturnType<typeof originalAppend>>` rather than `ReturnType<typeof originalAppend>` directly.

**This is safe because:**
- Callers of `sessionManager.appendMessage` are already in async contexts — they either `await` the result or discard it
- The TypeScript type for `SessionManager["appendMessage"]` accepts a Promise return; `tsc --noEmit` passes clean
- The public API surface (`installSessionToolResultGuard` signature) is unchanged

---

## Performance

The mutex adds one `Promise.resolve()` microtask chain per `appendMessage` call — approximately **0.01 ms overhead per call** in V8.

A typical tool-heavy agent turn (10 tool calls) incurs ~0.12 ms of added latency total. This is well below perception threshold and undetectable in any real workload benchmark.

The mutex does not slow the common path: in the correct (non-racing) case, concurrent callers would have executed sequentially anyway. The mutex prevents the _incorrect_ fast path where two handlers race to mutate `pending`.

> **Why not lock-free?** JavaScript is single-threaded within a Worker — "concurrency" here is cooperative (at `await` yield points), not preemptive. A promise-chain mutex is the idiomatic, zero-overhead solution. Lock-free data structures would add complexity with no benefit.

---

## Checklist

- [x] `tsc --noEmit` passes clean
- [x] 9/9 tests pass
- [x] New tests fail on unpatched code (regression gate confirmed)
- [x] No new npm dependencies
- [x] `installSessionToolResultGuard` public API unchanged
- [x] Closes #38432, #13351, #15008

target.title: fix(utils): reduce maskApiKey exposure from 16 to 4 characters
target.body:
## Summary

`maskApiKey()` exposes up to 16 characters of API keys when displaying `/models list` output in chat channels. For keys with 16 or fewer characters, the masked output may reveal most or all of the key.

**Before:**

```
sk-ant-api03-abc...wxyz1234   ← 16 chars exposed
abcdefghijklmnop              ← ab...op (4 chars exposed — may be most of key)
```

**After:**

```
sk-a...   ← only 4 chars, never the end
abcd...   ← only 4 chars, never the end
```

## Details

Simplified `maskApiKey()` to two branches:

- Keys ≤ 4 chars: show first 1 char + `...`
- Keys > 4 chars: show first 4 chars + `...`

The end of the key is **never** exposed. This follows the same pattern used by GitHub, npm, and other credential-handling systems.

Updated tests to match the new behavior and added assertions that verify the last 8 characters of a key are never present in masked output.

## Related Issues

Fixes #34452

## How to Validate

Run `/models list` in any channel — confirm API keys show only the first 4 characters followed by `...`.

Run unit tests: `pnpm test -- --testPathPattern=mask-api-key`

- [x] Added/updated tests (if needed)

target.title: fix: chat.abort RPC now stops embedded Pi agent, sub-agents, and session queues
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #38277

The `chat.abort` RPC handler only aborted HTTP streaming connections but did not stop the embedded Pi agent run, spawned sub-agents, or clear follow-up message queues. Clicking **Stop** in the webchat UI left the agent running and consuming tokens, unlike the `/stop` text command.

## Changes

- Added `abortEmbeddedPiRun(sessionId)`, `clearSessionQueues()`, and `stopSubagentsForRequester()` calls to both abort paths in `chat.abort` (by sessionKey and by runId)
- Both paths are guarded by `res.aborted` to avoid unnecessary work
- Added test file `chat.abort-embedded-run.test.ts` with 3 tests covering both paths + negative case

## How it was tested

- All 11 `chat.abort*` tests pass (3 new + 8 existing)
- Fix mirrors the existing correct implementation in `tryFastAbortFromMessage()` (used by `/stop`)


> This PR was developed with AI assistance.

target.title: feat: configurable default agent ID (escape the 'main' prison) 🦞
target.body:
## Summary

Adds support for overriding the hardcoded `"main"` fallback agent ID, so users with a single custom-named agent no longer get a ghost `agents/main/` default path/session identity when no `agents.list` is configured.

## The Problem

`DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` falls back to `"main"`. Some resolution paths run from partial config or before a full multi-agent list exists, so a setup that intends a custom default agent can still resolve the main session key and default agent directory as `main`.

## Solution

Two complementary override mechanisms:

### 1. Config key: `agents.defaultAgentId`

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaultAgentId": "maine-lobster"
  }
}
```

### 2. Env var: `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID`

```bash
export OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID=maine-lobster
```

### Priority (highest → lowest)

1. `agents.list[].default: true` (explicit default agent)
2. `agents.list[0]` (first configured agent)
3. `agents.defaultAgentId` config key
4. `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` env var
5. Hardcoded `"main"` (backward compatible)

## Changes

- Adds `agents.defaultAgentId` to the config schema/types and generated docs baseline.
- Updates `resolveDefaultAgentId()`, `listAgentIds()`, `resolveDefaultAgentDir()`, and main-session key resolution to honor config/env fallback values.
- Keeps populated `agents.list` behavior unchanged: list default/first entry still wins.
- Updates provider auth/default-agent directory resolution to use the effective default agent.
- Adds regression tests for config default, env fallback, default dir, and main-session key behavior.

## Testing

- `pnpm config:schema:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-scope-config.test.ts src/config/sessions/main-session.test.ts src/config/sessions/session-key.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD origin/main` → merge-clean

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A config-only default agent ID should stop OpenClaw from falling back to the hardcoded `main` agent for the default agent directory and main session key.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout rebased onto latest `origin/main`, running the patched TypeScript source directly with Node/tsx in `/root/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-pr-acpx/pr-51762`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```console
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import { resolveDefaultAgentId, resolveDefaultAgentDir } from './src/agents/agent-scope-config.ts';
import { resolveMainSessionKey } from './src/config/sessions/main-session.ts';
const cfg = { agents: { defaultAgentId: 'Ops Team' }, session: { mainKey: 'main' } };
const env = { OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR: '/tmp/openclaw-real-proof' };
console.log('defaultAgentId=' + resolveDefaultAgentId(cfg, env));
console.log('defaultAgentDir=' + resolveDefaultAgentDir(cfg, env));
console.log('mainSessionKey=' + resolveMainSessionKey(cfg, env));
EOF
```

- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output copied from the patched real source command above:

```console
defaultAgentId=ops-team
defaultAgentDir=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent
mainSessionKey=agent:ops-team:main
```

- **Observed result after fix**: `agents.defaultAgentId: "Ops Team"` is normalized to `ops-team`, the default agent directory resolves under `/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent`, and the main session key resolves to `agent:ops-team:main` instead of `main`.
- **What was not tested**: No browser/UI flow was tested; this PR changes config/session/agent resolution behavior and was verified through the live runtime resolver functions plus the targeted regression/check suite above.

Fixes #17477 🦞

target.title: fix: persist pending followup queues across gateway restart
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #51620 — gateway restart drops queued and in-flight inbound messages.

## Changes

### New: `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/persist.ts`
Serializes all non-empty `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` entries to `state/pending-messages.json` before shutdown. Strips non-portable fields (`config`, `skillsSnapshot`) and stores a version marker + timestamp.

### New: `src/gateway/server-pending-messages.ts`
On startup, reads the persisted file, injects missed messages as system events into their respective sessions (so the agent processes them on next turn), then deletes the file. Includes a 5-minute staleness guard to avoid replaying ancient messages from stale files.

### Modified: `src/gateway/server-close.ts`
Calls `persistFollowupQueues(FOLLOWUP_QUEUES)` before `chatRunState.clear()` so queue state is captured while still available. Best-effort — failure doesn't block shutdown.

### Modified: `src/gateway/server-startup.ts`
Calls `replayPersistedPendingMessages()` 1.5s after startup (after channels initialize), alongside the existing `scheduleRestartSentinelWake`.

## Tests

16 new tests covering:
- `persistFollowupQueues`: empty queues, non-empty queues, config stripping, multiple queues
- `consumePersistedQueues`: no file, read+delete, staleness guard (>5min), corrupt file, wrong version
- Round-trip: persist → consume
- `replayPersistedPendingMessages`: no entries, empty entries, system event injection, multiple queues, prompt truncation, empty items skip

## Limitations / Follow-up

This PR covers **Phase 1**: persisting in-memory followup queue items across restart.

Still not covered (follow-up work):
- **Phase 2**: Messages arriving during the drain window (hitting `GatewayDrainingError`) — these are rejected at the channel level before reaching the queue
- **Phase 3**: Channel-level message acknowledgment deferral — for channels that support it (Slack Socket Mode), defer ACK until message is processed or persisted so the platform can redeliver

## Testing

```
npx vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/queue/persist.test.ts src/gateway/server-pending-messages.test.ts
```

All 16 tests pass.

---

Upstream issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/51620

target.title: fix(#85334): prevent bundled plugin load paths from being injected into config
target.body:
## Background

`openclaw doctor --fix` was automatically adding bundled plugin paths (e.g., `memory-wiki`) to `plugins.load.paths` in `openclaw.json`. This produced a redundant-path warning on the next doctor run, creating a cycle: fix → adds path → warns → fix → adds path again.

## Changes

### A — `src/commands/onboarding-plugin-install.ts`

Added a bundled root guard in `addPluginLoadPath()`. Before writing a plugin path to `plugins.load.paths`, check whether it resolves to OpenClaw's bundled plugin directory via `resolvePackagedBundledLoadPathAlias` — the same function used by `discovery.ts` to emit the redundant-path warning. Bundled plugins are auto-discovered and do not need a config entry.

Non-bundled (external) plugins are unaffected.

### B — `src/commands/doctor/repair-sequencing.ts`

Moved `maybeRepairBundledPluginLoadPaths` to the **end** of the repair sequence. Previously it ran mid-sequence, but subsequent steps (`repairMissingConfiguredPluginInstalls`, `applyPluginAutoEnable`) could re-add the bundled path, leaving a dirty config to be persisted.

## Testing

- [ ] Mac mini: run `doctor --fix`, verify no redundant memory-wiki path in `plugins.load.paths`
- [ ] VPS: same test
- [ ] `doctor --fix` idempotent: second run produces same output

Closes #85334

target.title: feat(config): add llm shorthand config key for custom model providers
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a top-level `llm` shorthand key to `openclaw.json` so users can configure a custom model provider without manually editing `models.providers` + `agents.defaults`.
- Fixes the chat model picker sending a bare model ID (e.g. `deepseek-chat`) instead of the full `provider/model` ref, which caused sessions to resolve the model against the wrong default provider (e.g. `kimi/deepseek-chat`).

### Config example

```json
{
  "llm": {
    "provider": "openai-compatible",
    "model": "deepseek-chat",
    "api_key": "sk-xxx",
    "base_url": "https://api.deepseek.com/v1",
    "temperature": 0.7,
    "provider_id": "deepseek"
  }
}
```

This automatically expands into `models.providers` and sets `agents.defaults.model.primary` without overwriting existing config values.

## Changes

- `src/config/types.models.ts` — add `LlmConfig` type
- `src/config/zod-schema.core.ts` — add `LlmConfigSchema` with validation
- `src/config/types.openclaw.ts` — add `llm?: LlmConfig` field to `OpenClawConfig`
- `src/config/zod-schema.ts` — wire `LlmConfigSchema` into `OpenClawSchema`
- `src/config/defaults.ts` — add `applyLlmConfig()` pipeline step
- `src/config/io.ts` — call `applyLlmConfig` in all three config load paths
- `ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.ts` — fix model picker to use `provider/id` as option value

## Test plan

- [ ] Add `llm` block to `openclaw.json`, restart gateway, verify `models.providers` is populated and agent uses the configured model
- [ ] Verify existing `models.providers` entries are not overwritten when `llm` is also set
- [ ] Open chat model picker, select a non-default provider model, verify no `model not allowed: wrongprovider/model` error

target.title: fix(sandbox): fall back to static channel registry for sandbox explain channel resolution
target.body:
Fixes #51245

## Problem

`openclaw sandbox explain --session telegram:slash:12345` shows `channel: (unknown)` for Telegram slash sessions, causing elevated `allowFrom` checks to fail even when the Telegram sender is correctly allowlisted.

## Root Cause

`inferProviderFromSessionKey()` in `sandbox-explain.ts` uses `normalizeAnyChannelId()` which depends on the **runtime channel plugin registry**. When running from the CLI (`sandbox explain`), channel plugins may not be loaded, so built-in channels like `telegram` don't resolve.

## Fix

Fall back to the static `normalizeChatChannelId()` which uses the hardcoded `CHAT_CHANNEL_META` registry and always resolves built-in channels like `telegram`, `discord`, `slack`, etc.

## Test

```
pnpm test -- src/commands/sandbox-explain.test.ts
✓ 1 test passed
```

target.title: whatsapp: Allow different ack emoji for different agents
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `resolveAckReaction` in `src/agents/identity.ts` cast `channelCfg?.ackReaction` directly to `string` at L1/L2. WhatsApp's `ackReaction` is an object (`{ emoji?, direct?, group? }`), not a plain string — this would throw `channelReaction.trim is not a function` at runtime if called for WhatsApp. The WhatsApp extension therefore bypassed `resolveAckReaction` entirely, reading `ackConfig?.emoji` directly and losing the agent identity emoji fallback (L4).
- **Why it matters:** Multi-agent WhatsApp setups (e.g. two agents sharing one number via `bindings`) had no way to give each agent a distinct ack reaction emoji — the channel-level emoji was always used regardless of which agent was responding.
- **What changed:** Added `extractAckEmoji()` helper to handle both plain-string and object-with-emoji formats in L1/L2; exported `resolveAckReaction` from `channel-feedback.ts`; WhatsApp extension now uses `resolveAckReaction` so the agent identity emoji serves as a per-agent fallback.
- **What did NOT change:** Users with no `ackReaction` config continue to get no reaction (the `ackConfig !== undefined` guard preserves existing behaviour exactly).

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users with `channels.whatsapp.ackReaction` configured but no `emoji` field (e.g. `{ direct: true, group: "mentions" }`) will now see the responding agent's identity emoji used as the ack reaction instead of no reaction. Users with an explicit `emoji` value or no `ackReaction` config at all are unaffected.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22
- Integration/channel: WhatsApp

### Steps

1. Configure two agents with different identity emojis and a WhatsApp binding routing each agent to different senders
2. Remove `emoji` from `channels.whatsapp.ackReaction` (keep `direct`/`group` settings)
3. Send a WhatsApp message from each sender

### Expected

- Each agent ACKs with its own identity emoji

### Actual (before fix)

- No ack reaction sent (emoji resolved to empty string)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

4 new tests added to `src/agents/identity.test.ts` covering WhatsApp object-format at channel level, agent identity fallback when no emoji field, empty-emoji disable, and account-level object format. All 10 tests pass.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: existing `ackReaction: { emoji: "🍓" }` config unchanged; no config = no reaction unchanged; object without emoji = agent identity emoji used
- Edge cases checked: empty string emoji still disables reactions; account-level object format resolved correctly
- What you did not verify: live WhatsApp send (tested via unit tests and config inspection)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No — zero config changes needed to update
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/ack-reaction.ts` to read `ackConfig?.emoji` directly
- Files/config to restore: none required
- Known bad symptoms: unexpected ack reactions appearing for users with partial `ackReaction` config (no `emoji` field set)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: users with `ackReaction: {}` or `ackReaction: { direct: false }` (no emoji) start getting reactions from the agent identity emoji
  - Mitigation: this is an edge-case config (most users either set emoji or omit ackReaction entirely); the change is intentional and documented in User-visible Changes above

target.title: fix(infra): close file handles on lock write failure
target.body:
## Summary

File handles acquired by `fs.open()` in `acquireGatewayLock()` and `acquireFileLock()` leak when the subsequent `writeFile()` call throws.

## Problem

**Code path (gateway-lock.ts:198):**
1. `fs.open(lockPath, "wx")` succeeds → file handle acquired
2. `handle.writeFile(JSON.stringify(payload))` throws (disk full, I/O error, etc.)
3. Execution enters catch block → only EEXIST is handled, anything else throws `GatewayLockError`
4. The `release()` closure (which calls `handle.close()`) was never returned → **file descriptor leaked**
5. A partial/empty lock file remains on disk → may block future lock acquisition

**Same pattern in file-lock.ts:122:**
1. `fs.open(lockPath, "wx")` succeeds → file handle acquired
2. `handle.writeFile()` throws
3. Handle never reaches `HELD_LOCKS` map → `releaseHeldLock()` will never find it → **FD leaked**

## Proof of bug

The leak is provable by construction: `fs.open()` returns a `FileHandle` wrapping a kernel file descriptor. The only code path that closes it is the `release()` function, which is only reachable if writeFile succeeds and the handle is returned to the caller. On the writeFile failure path, no code references the handle again.

Under sustained lock contention with intermittent I/O errors (e.g. NFS mount flapping, disk quota exceeded), this could exhaust the process's FD limit (typically 1024 on Linux), causing cascading failures in unrelated subsystems.

## Fix

Wrap post-open operations in try/catch. On failure, close the handle and remove the partial lock file before propagating.

## Test plan

- [ ] Verify lock acquisition still works normally
- [ ] Verify lock release cleans up properly
- [ ] Existing lock tests pass without modification

Found by [gnosis-polyglot](https://github.com/forkjoin-ai/gnosis) topological scanner (RESOURCE_LEAK rule).

target.title: feat: add /interrupt command and cross-session thread context store
target.body:
## Summary

- **Closes #50554** — Adds `/interrupt <message>` command that aborts the current agent run and immediately re-queues the new instruction via `callGateway`, preserving session context so the agent can adapt without a full restart. Max message: 4,000 chars.
- **Closes #50556** — Adds `ThreadContextStore` that persists lightweight context records keyed by `channel/accountId/threadId`. When a user replies in an IM thread previously triggered by a cron/isolated-agent, the new session is bootstrapped with a prior-context note. Rolling cap of 200 entries, 7-day TTL.

## Test plan

- [ ] 7 unit tests for `/interrupt` command (parsing, auth, length limit, abort, gateway re-queuing)
- [ ] 12 unit tests for `ThreadContextStore` (round-trip, TTL pruning, max entries, formatting)
- [ ] Manual: `/interrupt <msg>` aborts active run and redirects with preserved session context
- [ ] Manual: cron-triggered reply to thread stores context; follow-up user reply in same thread shows prior-context note

target.title: fix(cli): recover replaced device approvals
target.body:
## Summary

- Recover `openclaw devices approve <requestId>` when a local same-device repair request supersedes the request being approved.
- Require the replacement to match the original device identity, role/client metadata, and a compatible scope superset before approving it via local fallback.
- Add focused infra and CLI tests for devices-first requestId churn, cron-first progressive authority, replacement approval, fail-closed cases, and JSON/interactive output.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/cli/devices-cli.test.ts src/infra/device-pairing-churn.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw devices approve <requestId>` no longer fails with an unclear `unknown requestId` when the same local CLI repair request has been superseded by a compatible replacement during the approval reconnect.

Real environment tested: Local source checkout on Linux after rebasing onto current `origin/main`; focused Vitest shards for CLI and infra pairing behavior.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm test src/cli/devices-cli.test.ts src/infra/device-pairing-churn.test.ts`

Evidence after fix: Test output reported `src/infra/device-pairing-churn.test.ts (2 tests)` passed and `src/cli/devices-cli.test.ts (43 tests)` passed; `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD` produced no output.

Observed result after fix: same-device compatible replacement approvals use the replacement request ID, emit resolved metadata in JSON mode, print an interactive replacement notice, and fail closed for missing snapshots, incompatible scopes, cross-device replacements, public-key changes, role changes, metadata conflicts, remote `--url`, or missing pending requests.

What was not tested: Full packaged Docker/Crabbox smoke for the final branch; previous black-box validation was used to define the focused source-level regression coverage.

Refs: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/74484

target.title: fix(heartbeat): suppress fallback after route-matched message tool
target.body:
When heartbeat runs in message-tool-only mode, a successful generic message tool delivery can still be followed by duplicate free-text fallback narration in the same Telegram chat.

Fixes #84217

## Affected surface

- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts`
  - Adds route-matched message tool delivery evidence for heartbeat replies only after the generic message tool sent to the same provider/target/account route.
- `src/auto-reply/reply-payload.ts`
  - Adds internal reply-payload metadata for route-scoped message tool delivery evidence.
- `src/auto-reply/heartbeat-reply-payload.ts`
  - Adds a heartbeat-specific evidence reader.
- `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts`
  - Suppresses free-text fallback only when the reply carries route-scoped message tool delivery evidence.
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.ts`
  - Leaves heartbeat payloads available for heartbeat-specific route evidence instead of dropping them via generic message-tool dedupe first.
- Tests:
  - `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.tool-response.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts`

## Scope

This is a parameter/evidence threading fix. It does not add a config flag, does not change heartbeat scheduling, and does not touch Slack identity, commitment, or channel-specific private paths.

The important semantic boundary is that "generic message tool delivery evidence" is not the same as "has outbound reply content". This PR only marks heartbeat replies when existing message-tool target evidence matches the same delivery route via the existing `resolveMessagingToolPayloadDedupe` route matcher. Global `messagingToolSentTexts` without route targets is intentionally not enough.

After Vincent's thread-route follow-up, I found one extra current-head issue while refreshing proof: generic reply-payload dedupe could drop the matched heartbeat payload before `heartbeat-runner` saw the route evidence. The current head keeps heartbeat payloads out of that generic dedupe layer so the heartbeat-specific suppression path receives the evidence and can still preserve no-route / wrong-topic fallback behavior.

## Prior art

Prior attempt #84273 was closed by contributor due to inability to provide live channel proof. This PR provides expanded source + runtime helper proof, with the helper semantic explicitly distinguishing "generic message tool delivery evidence" from "has outbound reply content" to address the message-delivery risk flagged in #84273.

The key difference from #84273 is route scope: #84273 marked heartbeat payloads after any message-tool send, while this PR only marks a heartbeat payload when committed `messagingToolSentTargets` match the reply route. If the message tool delivered to another route, or only global text evidence exists, heartbeat fallback still sends.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** heartbeat message-tool-only delivery should not send duplicate free-text fallback after the generic `message` tool has already delivered to the same route, but it must still send fallback when no same-route delivery evidence exists.
- **Real environment tested:** local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, Node 22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.0, branch `wolf/heartbeat-suppress-after-message-tool`, head `14b8854108`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran a local OpenClaw runtime helper that imports production `runHeartbeatOnce`, seeds a real `openclaw.json`-shaped config and session store, registers a local Telegram outbound adapter, then executes three heartbeat ticks: same-route evidence, no-route evidence, and wrong-thread/topic-unmarked evidence.
- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the runtime helper:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_PROOF_HEAD=14b8854108 pnpm exec tsx /tmp/openclaw-pr84975-proof/runtime-proof.mts
[runtime-helper-proof] repoHead=14b8854108
{
  "head": "14b8854108",
  "scenario": "same-route-message-tool-evidence-suppresses-fallback",
  "resultStatus": "ran",
  "routeEvidence": true,
  "telegramSendCount": 0,
  "eventStatus": "sent",
  "eventChannel": "telegram",
  "eventPreview": "Fallback narration that would duplicate same-route message-tool delivery."
}
{
  "head": "14b8854108",
  "scenario": "no-route-evidence-keeps-fallback-visible",
  "resultStatus": "ran",
  "routeEvidence": false,
  "telegramSendCount": 1,
  "sentTo": "<redacted-telegram-target>",
  "sentTextPreview": "Fallback narration still needs delivery without same-route evidence.",
  "eventStatus": "sent",
  "eventChannel": "telegram",
  "eventPreview": "Fallback narration still needs delivery without same-route evidence."
}
{
  "head": "14b8854108",
  "scenario": "wrong-thread-or-topic-unmarked-evidence-keeps-fallback-visible",
  "resultStatus": "ran",
  "routeEvidence": false,
  "telegramSendCount": 1,
  "sentTo": "<redacted-telegram-target>",
  "sentTextPreview": "Fallback narration still needs delivery for a different topic/thread.",
  "eventStatus": "sent",
  "eventChannel": "telegram",
  "eventPreview": "Fallback narration still needs delivery for a different topic/thread."
}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** with route-scoped message-tool evidence, `runHeartbeatOnce` completes and does not invoke the Telegram send path (`telegramSendCount: 0`), so the fallback narration is suppressed. Without route evidence, and for the wrong-topic/wrong-thread-unmarked fallback case, the same runtime path invokes the Telegram send path once (`telegramSendCount: 1`), proving fallback remains available when the message tool did not prove same-route delivery.
- **What was not tested:** I did not run a live Telegram heartbeat tick with a real bot token/chat. I did not test Slack/WhatsApp live channels; the patch is heartbeat reply evidence and generic runner fallback suppression, with Telegram-shaped runtime coverage for the reported duplicate free-text path.

## Supplemental validation

### RED before fix

The new regression was first run before the implementation. `runHeartbeatOnce` still sent fallback text even when the reply carried route-scoped message-tool delivery evidence:

```text
$ pnpm test src/infra/heartbeat-runner.tool-response.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts -t "message-tool delivery"
FAIL src/infra/heartbeat-runner.tool-response.test.ts > suppresses fallback text only after route-scoped message-tool delivery evidence
expected "spy" to not be called at all, but actually been called 1 times
received:
  ["-1001234567890", "Fallback narration that should not duplicate the message tool.", { verbose: false, cfg: [Object], accountId: undefined }]
```

### Current-head GREEN after fix

```text

Tests  9 passed | 53 skipped (62)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 37.25s

$ pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.test.ts -t "delivers distinct same-target replies when messageProvider is synthetic but originatingChannel is set"
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply.config.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 37 skipped (38)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 25.37s

### Previous changed gate

Push-before searches were rerun against open PRs:

target.title: fix(codex): deliver native spawn_agent completions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Codex-native `spawn_agent` children can finish successfully, but OpenClaw may not mirror their lifecycle, wake the requester session, or deliver the synthesized parent reply visibly back to Discord.
- Why it matters: Mission Control shows stale running task rows and users have to manually poke the parent agent before the completed child result appears.
- What changed: add a Codex-native subagent monitor, a gateway-side transcript/task reconciler, and a completion delivery adapter that turns native child transcript completions into durable parent completion handoffs.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not replace the broader subagent delivery architecture or implement ACP/native `sessions_spawn` ownership policy; it handles Codex harness native `spawn_agent` child threads and their durable transcript completions.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #44925
- Related #52249
- Related #83352
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex-native `spawn_agent` worker completion now wakes the parent Scout session and produces a visible Discord reply without a manual follow-up ping.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw native install on macOS, gateway LaunchAgent, Discord Mission Control channel, Codex harness native `spawn_agent`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Hot-patched the local installed OpenClaw gateway with the same source behavior in this PR.
  2. Restarted the gateway.
  3. From Discord `#mission-control`, asked the parent agent to spawn a worker to check weather using Codex native `spawn_agent`.
  4. Repeated after earlier failed attempts where the worker completed but the parent only replied after a manual poke.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
San Diego: 63°F and cloudy.

Notable forecast: Monday stays mostly cloudy with a high near 70°F, with SW wind gusts up to 20 mph.

Source: NOAA/NWS weather.gov, KSAN observation station.

End-to-end note: this reply is being sent visibly to <#1493670744029401119> from the subagent completion result.

Sent visibly to #mission-control.

Discord receipt: 1505787418349928589
```

Additional live test after packaging: a longer engineer analysis run completed and proactively returned the full worker response in Discord.

- Observed result after fix: the child worker completed, the parent Scout was re-entered without a user poke, and the final synthesized answer was sent visibly to the Discord Mission Control channel.
- What was not tested: Telegram/Slack/ACP paths, Docker install, and upstream CI before PR creation.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): repeated pre-fix live runs had completed worker results in the parent context, but the parent did not proactively reply until the user sent another Discord message.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Codex native `spawn_agent` owns the child thread lifecycle inside the Codex harness, so OpenClaw was not reliably converting child transcript terminal events into OpenClaw task completion and parent delivery state.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no durable gateway-side reconciler scanned Codex child transcripts for `event_msg.task_complete` / `task_failed` and retried parent completion delivery until the parent produced a visible response.
- Contributing context (if known): the old documented `sessions_spawn`/`sessions_yield` flow is not available in the Codex harness path; native `spawn_agent` produces usable child transcript state, but OpenClaw needs an adapter around it.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.test.ts`
  - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-notification.test.ts`
  - `src/plugin-sdk/codex-native-task-runtime.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: native Codex child notifications and transcript completions create/mirror task rows, finalize terminal rows, mark delivery pending, wake the requester parent, and only mark delivered after a durable direct parent completion response.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it tests the OpenClaw/Codex adapter seams without needing private Discord credentials or a live Codex websocket.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing subagent announce tests covered generic completion delivery but did not cover Codex-native child transcript reconciliation.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Codex-native delegated workers that complete in the background should now proactively deliver their completion back through the parent session, including visible Discord channel replies, instead of waiting for the user to poke the parent agent.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Discord parent -> Codex spawn_agent -> child transcript task_complete -> stale OpenClaw task / no proactive visible reply

After:
Discord parent -> Codex spawn_agent -> child transcript task_complete -> OpenClaw reconciler -> parent completion handoff -> visible Discord reply
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: native OpenClaw install, gateway LaunchAgent
- Model/provider: Codex harness, OpenAI Codex native `spawn_agent`
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord `#mission-control`
- Relevant config (redacted): Mission Control Discord channel session bound to Scout/parent agent

### Steps

1. From Discord `#mission-control`, ask the parent agent to spawn a worker using native `spawn_agent`.
2. Let the worker complete without sending a follow-up message.
3. Watch for a parent synthesized reply posted visibly in the same Discord channel.
4. Inspect task rows for `codex-thread:<child>` status and delivery status.

### Expected

- Worker completes.
- OpenClaw finalizes the mirrored `codex-native` task row.
- Parent Scout receives a completion handoff.
- Parent reply is sent visibly to Discord.
- Delivery is marked delivered only after the parent response path succeeds.

### Actual

- The live Discord weather test posted visibly to `#mission-control` with Discord receipt `1505787418349928589`.
- A later long engineer analysis run also completed and returned the full response proactively.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Codex-native worker completion posts a visible Discord parent reply after gateway restart; longer engineer analysis run also completed and proactively returned.
- Edge cases checked: stale terminal rows are not blindly replayed; delivery remains pending when the parent handoff does not produce a durable direct completion response; Discord/channel completion uses the message-tool-only path when required.
- What you did **not** verify: ACP runtime, Docker install, non-Discord channels, and full upstream CI.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: duplicate parent replies if a live notification and transcript reconciliation both observe the same child completion.
  - Mitigation: task delivery state and completion dedupe keys prevent delivered rows from being reconciled again; the reconciler ignores already delivered/failed rows and old terminal rows outside the active/pending window.
- Risk: parent transcript completion exists but external channel delivery still fails.
  - Mitigation: Codex-native completions are treated as agent-mediated completion sources and require durable direct parent completion delivery before marking delivered.

target.title: fix(plugin-loader): prefer native loading for compiled SDK imports
target.body:
## Summary

This changes the plugin module loader to try native loading before forcing `jiti` source transformation for compiled JavaScript files that import `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*`.

The loader still preserves the existing safety fallback: if native loading declines and the file needs plugin-sdk alias rewriting, it falls back to the alias-aware `jiti` path.

## Why

On a 2026.5.18 install, `openclaw infer audio providers --json` spent most of its cold-start time loading the Codex plugin through `jiti` even though the plugin is already compiled JS.

Measured locally:

- Current live install: `openclaw infer audio providers --json` ~35-38s
- `OPENCLAW_PLUGIN_LOAD_PROFILE=1`: Codex discovery alone ~25-30s
- CPU profile: dominated by `jiti` loading/transformation under `@openclaw/codex/dist/...`
- Direct native import of `@openclaw/codex/dist/index.js`: ~3.6s

With this patch applied to a throwaway copy of the installed runtime:

- `infer audio providers --json`: ~12.8s
- `infer audio transcribe` on the same 5.48s OGG test file: ~15.5s
- Actual hosted OpenAI transcription request was already ~1.7-2.8s, so this primarily reduces local/plugin-loader overhead.

## Tests

- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/plugin-module-loader-cache.test.ts`

target.body:
## Summary

- broadcast returned `isError` agent-run payloads as terminal `chat` error events after an agent has started
- mark the `chat:<runId>` dedupe entry failed for those returned error payloads
- add a regression covering post-agent-start idle-timeout-style error delivery without mirroring assistant transcript entries

Fixes #84945.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts` (2 files, 152 tests passed)
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts CHANGELOG.md`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: returned `isError` payloads after `agentRunStarted=true` were persisted/logged but not broadcast as terminal chat errors.
Real environment tested: local Gateway server-method unit harness in a Codex worktree, with `dispatchInboundMessage` triggering `onAgentRunStart` and returning a final error payload.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`.
Evidence after fix: the new regression observes a `chat` event with `state: "error"`, `errorMessage: "LLM idle timeout (120s): no response from model"`, and a failed `chat:<runId>` dedupe snapshot.
Observed result after fix: connected Gateway clients receive the terminal error event while normal agent-run final text still is not mirrored into assistant transcript entries.
What was not tested: a live ACP/Ki-Agents 120s idle timeout run; CI and the focused Gateway harness cover the source-level failure path.
