target.title: fix(gateway): hold startup-gated requests at server until post-attach (closes #67160)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** The openclaw-gateway startup gate from #65365 responds `UNAVAILABLE` to startup-gated methods (`chat.history`, `models.list`, `sessions.*`, `tools.effective`, `agent.wait`) for the 8–15 s window between `[gateway] ready` and post-attach sidecar registration. The Control UI's client-side retry loop (same commit) only helps when the browser already has the retry-aware bundle. Every time an update restarts the gateway, an open browser tab is still running the **pre-fix** bundle, fires one `chat.history`, gets `UNAVAILABLE`, never retries, and pins the red `GatewayRequestError: chat.history unavailable during gateway startup` banner.
- **Why it matters:** Every in-place `openclaw-update` hits this for any user with a chat tab open — which is most of the time.
- **What changed:** The gateway now *holds* gated requests on the server until post-attach finishes, via a small `StartupGateBarrier` (deferred + 20 s timeout) threaded through the request context. If post-attach completes within the wait, the request falls through to the normal handler. If it times out, the existing `retryable/retryAfterMs` `UNAVAILABLE` shape is returned as a fallback. This removes the race independently of what bundle any browser tab has cached.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** UI is untouched. Upstream's declarative startup-gated method set (`STARTUP_UNAVAILABLE_GATEWAY_METHODS`, derived from `startup: true` core descriptors) is left exactly as upstream defines it — this PR adds only the server-side wait, not a competing method list.

## Re-port note (refreshed 2026-05-16)

This PR was originally written on 2026-04-17. Upstream has since restructured the startup gate: `src/gateway/server-startup-unavailable-methods.ts` was **deleted** and the gated method set is now declarative in `src/gateway/methods/core-descriptors.ts`. The change has been **re-architected (not rebased)** onto current `upstream/main` (`210ff7d318`):

- The barrier now lives in a new dedicated `src/gateway/server-startup-gate-barrier.ts` (its previous home no longer exists upstream).
- Upstream's `STARTUP_UNAVAILABLE_GATEWAY_METHODS` is consumed as-is; no hand-maintained list is reintroduced.
- The fail-fast gate in `src/gateway/server-methods.ts` now awaits the barrier, preserving upstream's `GATEWAY_STARTUP_RETRY_AFTER_MS` + `gatewayStartupUnavailableDetails()` response shape on timeout.
- `startupGateBarrier` is threaded as an **optional** field on the post-attach and request-context params, so existing test call sites need no changes and legacy contexts keep the immediate-UNAVAILABLE behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #67160
- Related #65322, #65365, #67951
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The startup gate's fail-fast response (`UNAVAILABLE` at 0 ms) makes the race unrecoverable from the client side when the browser-cached UI bundle predates the retry fix in #65365. The retry fix can never help the session that's affected by the update that ships it.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** The gate was designed around "short-lived unavailability the client can retry through." It did not account for long-lived cached bundles that lack the retry code.
- **Contributing context:** #65365 added the gate + client retry in the same commit, so the retry always ships one restart behind the bundle that needs it.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level:
  - [x] Unit test
- **Target test or file:** new `src/gateway/server-methods.startup-gate.test.ts` (unit), exercising the dispatch seam directly via `handleGatewayRequest`.
- **Scenarios locked in:**
  1. A gated request waits on the barrier and dispatches once the barrier opens (post-attach completes).
  2. A gated request responds with retryable `UNAVAILABLE` only if the barrier does not open within `STARTUP_GATE_WAIT_MS` (20 s).
  3. Non-gated methods bypass the wait entirely.
  4. Contexts without a barrier (legacy) preserve the existing immediate-UNAVAILABLE behavior.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** the four dispatch behaviors are exercised directly through `handleGatewayRequest` with a real `createStartupGateBarrier()`; the post-attach release point is a single `barrier.open()` call adjacent to the existing (already-tested) `unavailableGatewayMethods` clear, so it needs no separate harness.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: During the window between `[gateway] ready` and post-attach sidecar registration, the gateway answered startup-gated methods (`chat.history`, `models.list`) with an instant `UNAVAILABLE`. A browser tab still on the prior bundle fires one `chat.history`, gets `UNAVAILABLE`, never retries, and pins the red error banner after every gateway restart. This change holds the request on the server until post-attach instead of failing fast.
- **Real environment tested**: Live `openclaw-gateway` on aarch64 Linux (Snapdragon, WSL2) — real `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway` capture across an `openclaw-update`-triggered restart with a Control UI browser tab open on the prior bundle. Plus the compiled `createStartupGateBarrier()` from this commit driven directly under `node` on the same host.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Restart `openclaw-gateway` with a Control UI tab already open on the prior bundle and follow `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway`; separately, run the compiled `createStartupGateBarrier()` from this commit under `node` and drive `waitWithTimeout()` across the timeout, post-attach-`open()`, and already-open paths.
- **Before evidence optional**:

  ```
  05:22:24.767    [ws] webchat connected … client=openclaw-control-ui webchat v2026.4.11
  05:22:24.783    [ws] ⇄ res ✗ chat.history  0ms errorCode=UNAVAILABLE
  05:22:24.796    [ws] ⇄ res ✗ models.list   0ms errorCode=UNAVAILABLE
  ```

  One request per method, no retries — the cached bundle lacked retry code; red banner pins until reload.
- **Evidence after fix**:

  Real gateway journal, restart-while-tab-open — the server now holds the gated calls ~11 s, then dispatches; zero `UNAVAILABLE`:

  ```
  07:19:26.520    [gateway] ready (10.0s)
  07:19:26.580    [gateway] starting channels and sidecars...
  07:19:30.844    [ws] webchat connected … client=openclaw-control-ui webchat v2026.4.16
  07:19:42.024    [ws] ⇄ res ✓ chat.history 11172ms
  07:19:42.027    [ws] ⇄ res ✓ models.list  11162ms
  07:19:42.111    [plugins] embedded acpx runtime backend ready
  ```

  Live `node` console output of this commit's compiled barrier:

  ```
  WAIT_MS 20000 isOpen false
  timeout path => false (~121ms)   # held, retryable fallback only after the wait
  open path    => true  (~51ms)    # post-attach open() releases the held request
  already-open => true             # post-attach-complete fast path
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: The gated requests are held on the server for ~11.2 s (the exact post-attach window) and then dispatched to the real handler — zero `✗ UNAVAILABLE` rows, no red banner. The compiled barrier holds until `open()` and only falls back to the retryable `UNAVAILABLE` on timeout, matching the live gateway behavior end-to-end.
- **What was not tested**: The gateway journal is from the original landing of this identical barrier code path; this commit re-architects only where the barrier module lives and how it wires into upstream's restructured startup descriptors, not its runtime behavior — which is re-exercised live above under `node`. A fresh production restart-while-tab-open journal for the re-architected wiring will be captured at deploy via the normal `openclaw-update` flow; a maintainer may apply the `proof:` override if a fresh pre-merge capture is preferred.

## Supplemental verification (not a substitute for the proof above)

- `pnpm build` — clean.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:core:test` — clean (full typecheck of sources and tests).
- `src/gateway/server-methods.startup-gate.test.ts` — 4/4 passing (gated→wait→dispatch, timeout→UNAVAILABLE, non-gated passthrough, legacy no-barrier).
- `pnpm check:madge-import-cycles` / `check:import-cycles` — 0 cycles (the barrier module is a zero-import leaf).

## AI disclosure

- [x] AI-assisted (designed and implemented in Claude Code).
- Real behavior proof: the original real-gateway before/after journal (same barrier code path) plus live runtime console output of this commit's compiled barrier — see "Real behavior proof" above. A fresh production restart-while-tab-open journal for the re-architected wiring is captured at deploy time per the scope note; build/typecheck/unit/cycle checks are supplemental only.

target.title: Fix delivery-ready verification blockers
target.body:
## Summary

This PR closes the remaining delivery-ready verification blockers for the current branch.

It completes three founder-visible fixes:
- suppress non-actionable historical task delivery warning noise on operator surfaces
- normalize heartbeat `0m` semantics across status and health surfaces
- improve async exec approval followup messaging so user-visible replies stop leaking raw internal execution metadata

It also fixes the final type-surface issues discovered during fresh closure verification.

## What changed

### Task registry / operator surface
- stop surfacing already-non-actionable historical succeeded tasks as actionable delivery failure noise
- preserve stored task history while cleaning the reconciled read/audit surface

### Heartbeat status consistency
- align `0m` heartbeat semantics across `status --deep` and `health --json`
- remove contradictory reporting between status surfaces

### Async exec followup messaging
- prefer internal continuation plus synthesized user-facing results
- avoid leaking raw gateway/session/exit metadata into normal chat replies

### Closure verification fixes
- fix the type/test-surface issues found during fresh `pnpm check`
- keep the final closure edits minimal and scoped to the failing surfaces

## Verification

Fresh verification on this branch:
- `pnpm lint` ✅
- `pnpm check` ✅
- `pnpm build` ✅

Previously targeted verification for the repaired slices also passed:
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-approval-followup.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/commands/health.command.coverage.test.ts src/commands/status-all/diagnosis.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/doc-baseline.integration.test.ts test/release-check.test.ts test/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.test.ts` ✅

## Notes

An earlier assumption that repo-wide extension lint debt was the remaining blocker did not hold under fresh verification. The real final blockers were a small set of type-surface failures, which are fixed here.

target.title: fix: prevent toLowerCase error when model.id or model.name is undefined
target.body:
## Summary
Fixes the "f.toLowerCase is not a function" error that occurs in the agent:bootstrap hook when model.id or model.name is undefined.

## Root Cause
In `canonicalizeLegacyResolvedModel()` (src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.ts), the code called `.trim().toLowerCase()` on `params.model.id` and `params.model.name` without first checking if they are strings. When these properties are undefined (which can happen with certain model configurations), it causes a TypeError.

## Fix
Added `typeof` checks before calling string methods:
- Check `typeof params.model.id === "string"` before calling trim/toLowerCase
- Check `typeof params.model.name === "string"` before calling trim/toLowerCase

## Test Plan
- [x] Existing unit tests pass (src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.inline-provider.test.ts)
- [x] Lint checks pass

Closes openclaw#68124

target.title: fix(cron): prevent scheduler death when startup catch-up fails
target.body:
## Summary

When runMissedJobs() throws during start(), the timer was never armed, silently killing the cron scheduler until the next gateway restart.

## Root Cause

The start() function in src/cron/service/ops.ts calls runMissedJobs() outside of any try/catch, followed by the locked() block that calls armTimer(state). If runMissedJobs() throws (e.g. an isolated agent turn fails during startup catch-up), execution never reaches armTimer(), and the scheduler is permanently dead.

## Changes

1. **Guard armTimer in start()** - Wrap runMissedJobs() in try/catch so the timer is always armed
2. **Zombie detection in onTimer()** - Clear stale runningAtMs markers at runtime (threshold: 2x job timeout or 2 min) so jobs recover without restart
3. **Tests** - Verify timer stays armed on catch-up failure and stale markers are cleared

Fixes #67854
Related: #59056, #60799

## Summary
- stop treating workflow-looking filenames as inline pipelines when resolution fails

target.title: mattermost: fix /oc_* slash command 503 from duplicate slash-state instance
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: Mattermost slash commands (`/oc_model`, `/oc_status`, `/oc_help`, …) always return `503 Service Unavailable` with `{"response_type":"ephemeral","text":"Slash commands are not yet initialized. Please try again in a moment."}`, even after the log confirms `mattermost: slash commands activated for account default (8 commands)`.
- **Why it matters**: On macOS/Linux installs, every \`/oc_*\` slash command in Mattermost is broken, with no path to recovery short of patching the installed npm package. Slack, Discord, etc. are unaffected.
- **What changed**: `extensions/mattermost/index.ts` now imports `registerSlashCommandRoute` statically from `./slash-route-api.js` instead of resolving it through the sync jiti loader `loadBundledEntryExportSync`. That makes the plugin-registration path and the monitor path share a single ESM instance of `slash-state.ts`, so the HTTP route closure and `activateSlashCommands` see the same `accountStates` map.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: No changes to `slash-state.ts`, `monitor-slash.ts`, `monitor.ts`, the HTTP handler, slash command registration flow, or any other extension. Just the single import in `extensions/mattermost/index.ts`. Slack's `extensions/slack/index.ts` has a similar-looking dual-path import, but its route handler does not close over module state (the handler uses `await import("./handler.runtime.js")` inside), so Slack is not affected by the same bug and is explicitly out of scope here.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

`extensions/mattermost/index.ts::registerFull` used `loadBundledEntryExportSync(import.meta.url, { specifier: "./slash-route-api.js", exportName: "registerSlashCommandRoute" })` to load the route registrar. On macOS/Linux that helper resolves through **jiti** (see `src/plugin-sdk/channel-entry-contract.ts`: `nodeRequire` is only tried inside the Win32 branch, everywhere else falls through to `getJiti(modulePath)(modulePath)`).

`slash-route-api.ts` re-exports `registerSlashCommandRoute` from `./src/mattermost/slash-state.js`. Loading it via jiti causes jiti to re-evaluate `slash-state.ts` inside its own module registry, producing a second live instance of the module. Meanwhile `src/mattermost/monitor-slash.ts` and `src/mattermost/monitor.ts` (and therefore the channel runtime loaded via `await import("./channel.runtime-*.js")`) import `./slash-state.js` through native ESM, giving them instance A.

The result: two separate `accountStates` maps.

- `activateSlashCommands` / `deactivateSlashCommands` mutate the **ESM** copy → the log line "slash commands activated for account default (N commands)" fires on the ESM instance.
- `registerSlashCommandRoute`'s `routeHandler` closure closes over the **jiti** copy's `accountStates` → `accountStates.size` stays `0` for the lifetime of the process → every callback returns `503 "Slash commands are not yet initialized. Please try again in a moment."`.

This matches the guidance in `src/plugin-sdk/CLAUDE.md`:

> Do not mix static and dynamic imports for the same runtime surface when shaping SDK seams.

The fix is the straightforward application of that rule: `index.ts` should import `./slash-route-api.js` statically so `slash-state.ts` loads through native ESM exactly once, shared with the monitor / channel-runtime path.

### Missing detection / guardrail

There is a good unit test for the token-routing logic in `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-state.test.ts`, but it only exercises one import of `slash-state`, so it cannot detect the dual-instance bug. I am happy to add a regression test that specifically simulates loading `slash-route-api.js` through jiti alongside the native ESM import and asserts that activation flows through to the route handler — happy to do it in this PR if maintainers prefer. I did not include it in the initial patch because simulating the jiti boundary in Vitest is a bit more involved than the fix itself, and I wanted the minimal-risk diff to land first.

### Contributing context

The same `loadBundledEntryExportSync` pattern is used in `extensions/feishu/index.ts`, `extensions/nostr/index.ts`, `extensions/qqbot/index.ts`, `extensions/slack/index.ts`, `extensions/zalouser/index.ts`. Slack is safe because its handler does `await import("./handler.runtime.js")` inside the request closure instead of closing over module state. I did not audit the others in depth; if similar closures-over-module-state exist there, they would have the same failure mode and warrant a follow-up. Flagging for visibility, not proposing to change them in this PR.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Seam / integration test
- **Target test or file**: `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-state.dual-load.test.ts` (new) — would load `slash-route-api.ts` through a jiti instance and `slash-state.ts` through native ESM in the same test, activate commands on the ESM side, then drive an HTTP request against the jiti-registered route and assert that it does **not** return 503.
- **Scenario to lock in**: "route handler registered via `loadBundledEntryExportSync` observes activations performed via the monitor's static import of `slash-state.js`."
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail**: The bug is entirely a module-identity bug; pure-unit tests that import `slash-state.ts` once do not reproduce it. A boundary test that crosses the same jiti / ESM seam is the minimum.
- **Existing test that already covers this**: None — `slash-state.test.ts` is single-import only.
- **If no new test is added, why not**: I did not want to block the fix on writing the jiti-boundary harness; happy to add it as a follow-up or in this PR if a reviewer prefers.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Mattermost `/oc_status`, `/oc_model`, `/oc_models`, `/oc_new`, `/oc_help`, `/oc_think`, `/oc_reasoning`, `/oc_verbose` (and any user-defined slash commands registered through the same path) start working again instead of responding `503 Service Unavailable / "Slash commands are not yet initialized."`.
- No config changes. No new permissions. The HTTP route path, callback URL behavior, 401/409/413 responses, and multi-account dispatch logic are all unchanged.

## Diagram

```text
Before (broken):

  registerFull(api)
    └─ loadBundledEntryExportSync(...) ──(jiti)──► slash-state.ts   (instance B)
                                                     └─ accountStates_B  ◄── routeHandler closure
                                                                                 (size always 0 → 503)

  channel.runtime.ts ──(native ESM import)──► slash-state.ts   (instance A)
                                                 └─ accountStates_A  ◄── activateSlashCommands writes here


After (fixed):

  registerFull(api)
    └─ import { registerSlashCommandRoute } from "./slash-route-api.js"
                                    │
                                    ▼
                              slash-state.ts   (single ESM instance)
                                    └─ accountStates  ◄── routeHandler AND activateSlashCommands
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` — slash-command dispatch semantics, token validation, and multi-account routing are unchanged. The fix only restores the previously-intended flow.
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.3.x (Darwin 25.3.0, arm64)
- Runtime/container: Node 22.22.0, openclaw installed via `npm i -g openclaw@2026.4.15`, gateway started as the launchd `ai.openclaw.gateway` LaunchAgent.
- Integration/channel: Mattermost 11.5.1 (team edition) behind Caddy, multiple `channels.mattermost.accounts.*` entries (default / forge / kline / probe / quill / scout) but only the `default` account has team-admin permission to register commands, so `accountStates.size === 1` — the most common production shape.

### Steps

1. Gateway boots; logs show:
   ```
   [plugins] mattermost: registered slash command callback at /api/channels/mattermost/command
   [mattermost] slash commands activated for account default (8 commands)
   [mattermost] slash commands registered (8 commands across 1 teams, callback=http://<lan>:18789/api/channels/mattermost/command)
   ```
2. Trigger `/oc_model` from the Mattermost UI. Server also reproducible via:
   ```
   curl -sS -o /dev/null -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" -X POST \
     http://127.0.0.1:18789/api/channels/mattermost/command \
     -d 'token=<real-token>&command=/oc_model'
   ```

### Expected

- For a single-account setup, the `size === 1` branch should dispatch straight to `state.handler`, which validates the token internally and returns the normal slash-command response (200 with the ephemeral reply, or a 401 only for a genuinely wrong token).

### Actual (before the fix)

- `HTTP 503` with body `{"response_type":"ephemeral","text":"Slash commands are not yet initialized. Please try again in a moment."}`, even though the activation log fired seconds earlier. The Mattermost UI surfaces this as `触发器 "oc_model" 返回了响应状态 503 Service Unavailable.` / "Trigger \"oc_model\" returned response status 503 Service Unavailable."

## Evidence

Failing-before / passing-after against a locally patched installed dist (equivalent rewrite of this TS change onto the bundled output, `/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/extensions/mattermost/index.js`):

Before (immediately after `launchctl kickstart -k gui/$UID/ai.openclaw.gateway`, well past activation):

```
$ curl -sS -o /dev/null -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" -X POST \
    http://127.0.0.1:18789/api/channels/mattermost/command \
    -d 'token=foo&command=/oc_model'
HTTP 503
$ curl -sS -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18789/api/channels/mattermost/command \
    -d 'token=foo&command=/oc_model'
{"response_type":"ephemeral","text":"Slash commands are not yet initialized. Please try again in a moment."}
```

After applying the equivalent patch and restarting the gateway:

```
$ curl -sS -o /dev/null -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" -X POST \
    http://127.0.0.1:18789/api/channels/mattermost/command \
    -d 'token=foo&command=/oc_model'
HTTP 400
$ curl -sS -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18789/api/channels/mattermost/command \
    -d 'token=foo&command=/oc_model'
{"response_type":"ephemeral","text":"Invalid slash command payload."}
$ curl -sS -o /dev/null -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" -X POST \
    http://127.0.0.1:18789/api/channels/mattermost/command \
    --data-urlencode 'token=not-a-real-token' \
    --data-urlencode 'command=/oc_model' \
    --data-urlencode 'team_id=498ogwtouigf8xfbuks451e8aa' \
    --data-urlencode 'channel_id=c' --data-urlencode 'user_id=u' \
    --data-urlencode 'user_name=n' --data-urlencode 'text=' \
    --data-urlencode 'trigger_id=t'
HTTP 401
```

Interpretation: the `503 "not yet initialized"` path is now unreachable — incoming requests flow into the real handler, which correctly rejects malformed payloads (400) and bad tokens (401). A real Mattermost-signed request with the registered token returns the normal slash-command response.

Relevant gateway log lines from the fixed run (unchanged shape, included for completeness):

```
[plugins] mattermost: registered slash command callback at /api/channels/mattermost/command
[mattermost] [default] starting channel
[mattermost] connected as @nova
[mattermost] command /oc_model already registered (id=fffuot5kpjgafkmx7rc5fk9xjc)
[mattermost] slash commands activated for account default (8 commands)
[mattermost] slash commands registered (8 commands across 1 teams, callback=http://<lan>:18789/api/channels/mattermost/command)
```

## Human Verification

- **Verified scenarios**:
  - Single-account (`accountStates.size === 1`) dispatch path, which was the production failure.
  - End-to-end via the running `ai.openclaw.gateway` LaunchAgent and real Mattermost (`/oc_model` from the Mattermost UI).
  - 400 / 401 paths reached correctly with synthetic curl payloads.
- **Edge cases checked**:
  - Restart via `launchctl kickstart -k` between before/after so the Node process and jiti registry are both replaced.
  - Confirmed that the fix does not regress the registered-HTTP-route path set for known `callbackPath` / `callbackUrl` overrides (route registration log is unchanged).
- **What I did NOT verify**:
  - Multi-account (`accountStates.size > 1`) token-routing path — code path is untouched, but I did not have a second team-admin account to activate commands on. The unit tests in `slash-state.test.ts` cover that branch.
  - `pnpm check` / `pnpm build` / `pnpm test` — I did not run these locally before opening the PR (this is my first contribution to the repo and I wanted to avoid a large-deps install cycle before you tell me which gates you want covered). Please run CI; happy to iterate on any failures or add the jiti-boundary regression test if that is what you would like to see.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: `slash-route-api.ts` and transitively `./src/mattermost/slash-state.ts` now load eagerly as part of the plugin entry, instead of only when `registerFull` fires through jiti. `slash-state.ts` is a small module (≈300 LOC, top-level state is just one `Map`), but this does add it to the mattermost plugin's cold-start import cost.
  - **Mitigation**: the module has no expensive side effects at import time (just a `new Map()` and function definitions), matches how `monitor.ts` already imports it on the hot monitor path, and aligns with the SDK guidance that says the eager/light contract belongs next to the plugin entry and only the heavy runtime should live behind a lazy `*.runtime.ts` boundary. I am happy to instead introduce a dedicated `slash-route.runtime.ts` and keep `registerSlashCommandRoute` lazy-but-single-instance if maintainers prefer that shape.
- **Risk**: Other bundled channels use the same `loadBundledEntryExportSync` pattern and may have similar dual-instance bugs depending on whether their route registrars close over module state.
  - **Mitigation**: Out of scope for this PR — Slack is demonstrably fine. Happy to open a follow-up audit if useful.

Reproducibility: yes. for the patch defects: source inspection shows the missing session-id path and stale wrapper integration, and Node Headers probes reproduce invalid-value failures and duplicate header normalization.
commands.review_followup: The remaining blockers are localized code, package, and test repairs that an automated repair lane can attempt after rebasing the branch.

target.title: feat(providers/zai): inject X-Session-Id header for prompt cache stickiness
target.body: ## Summary Add `X-Session-Id` header injection for the `zai` provider to enable sticky routing on ZhipuAI's backend load balancer. On a live agent workload (78 GLM-5.1 calls in a single session), this lifts prompt-cache hit rate from **~52% → ~96%** without any user-facing configuration change. Per ZhipuAI support team guidance, sending a stable `X-Session-Id` header per logical session causes the backend LB to route all requests in that session to the same inference node — which aligns prompt-cache residency with the user's actual session boundaries instead of letting LB variance thrash the cache. ## Motivation On tool-heavy multi-turn agent workloads, the system prompt and tool definitions dominate the prompt tokens and remain stable across calls, so they *should* live in the provider's prompt cache. But without a stable session key, ZhipuAI's backend LB may route successive calls from the same logical session to different inference nodes, each of which has to rebuild the prefix cache independently. The observable symptom is a sawtooth per-call hit-rate pattern that plateaus far below the ceiling. This PR mirrors the same "header wrapper" shape already in use for the `xai` provider (`extensions/xai/stream.ts`), so the change is idiomatic and low-risk. ## Design Added `extensions/zai/stream.ts`: - **`createZaiSessionIdHeaderWrapper(baseStreamFn?)`** — stream wrapper that reads `options.sessionId`, validates it, and injects `X-Session-Id` into the outbound headers. When `sessionId` is missing, empty, whitespace-only, or exceeds 256 chars, the wrapper passes through unchanged (no header injected). - **`wrapZaiProviderStream(ctx)`** — provider-level composition. Composes the session-id header wrapper with the existing `tool-stream-default-on` behavior via `composeProviderStreamWrappers` + `createToolStreamWrapper`. Shape mirrors `extensions/xai/stream.ts`. `extensions/zai/index.ts` now registers the provider with `wrapStreamFn: wrapZaiProviderStream` instead of spreading `ZAI_TOOL_STREAM_HOOKS`, preserving the existing tool-stream behavior while adding the header injection. ## Visual comparison Same task (LinkedIn jobs research, multi-turn browser agent) on GLM-5.1, observed end-to-end: **Before PR** (orange, 50 calls): per-call hit rate is sawtoothed between ~8% and ~99%; cumulative rate plateaus at **52.4%**. 24 of 50 calls land in the "cold" bucket (hit < 10%), indicating routing-driven cache misses recur throughout the session, not just at the start. **After PR** (green, 78 calls): per-call hit rate climbs to 95-99% within the first 2-3 calls and stays essentially flat for the rest of the session; cumulative rate converges to **96.27%**. Only **1** cold call (the mandatory first-call warm-up). Two `×` markers indicate network errors that short-circuited usage accounting — unrelated to this change. ## Test plan ### Unit tests (`extensions/zai/stream.test.ts`, 13 tests, all passing) - sessionId happy path (injects `X-Session-Id` into outgoing headers) - sessionId preserves other headers passed in options - sessionId === `null`, `undefined`, empty string, whitespace-only → no header injected - sessionId > 256 chars → no header injected (length guard) - sessionId containing unicode / special characters → preserved verbatim - `wrapZaiProviderStream` composition with `tool_stream: true` and `tool_stream: false` - `createZaiSessionIdHeaderWrapper` with and without explicit `baseStreamFn` ``` ✓ extension-providers extensions/zai/stream.test.ts (13 tests) 16ms Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 13 passed (13) ``` The pre-existing `cache-ttl.test.ts` (added in #24541) still passes, confirming no regression on the cache-TTL eligibility path that zai shares with moonshot. ### Live benchmark Identical agent task run under two configurations: | Metric | Before (no X-Session-Id) | After (this PR) | |------------------------------|-------------------------:|----------------:| | Calls (successful / errored) | 50 / 0 | 76 / 2 (network) | | Total prompt tokens | 1,589,843 | 2,672,479 | | Cached (hit) tokens | 832,896 | 2,572,736 | | Uncached (miss) tokens | 756,947 | 99,743 | | **Cache hit rate (aggregate)** | **52.39%** | **96.27%** | | **Median per-call hit rate** | **79.35%** | **99.11%** | | Cold calls (hit < 10%) | 24 / 50 | 1 / 76 | | Unique session IDs | 50 (per-request UUIDs) | 1 (session-level) | ### Cost impact Assuming GLM-5.1 cached tokens bill at ~20% of the input rate, effective input-token cost drops from ~0.58× nominal to ~0.23× nominal on this workload — **roughly a 60% input-token cost reduction**. ## Alternatives considered ### Why not rely on pi-ai's `sendSessionAffinityHeaders` compat gate? `pi-ai@0.69.0` ships a generic session-affinity mechanism in `providers/openai-completions.js` that, when `compat.sendSessionAffinityHeaders === true`, injects three headers — `session_id`, `x-client-request-id`, and `x-session-affinity` — on OpenAI-completions requests. On paper this would cover the zai transport without any plugin-local wrapper. In practice it doesn't help for this PR: 1. **The gate is default-off and currently unreached.** `detectCompat` hardcodes `sendSessionAffinityHeaders: false` for every provider. Neither pi-ai itself nor `openclaw/main` contains a single write that sets it to `true` — both `grep -rn 'sendSessionAffinityHeaders:\s*true' node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-ai/dist` and `git grep sendSessionAffinityHeaders origin/main` return no matches. Turning it on for zai would be a separate decision in its own right. 2. **The header names don't match.** The three headers above use snake_case `session_id` plus hyphenated `x-*` variants. ZhipuAI's support team specified `X-Session-Id` (capital X, capital S, capital I, hyphen-separated) as the exact sticky-routing key their load balancer matches on. We can't assume the LB accepts the pi-ai variants. 3. **Scope.** Flipping the compat gate would also change retry, WebSocket warmup, and any other OpenAI-compatible provider that later opts in. Keeping the injection plugin-local via `wrapStreamFn` confines the blast radius to `zai` and to the literal `X-Session-Id` key. If ZhipuAI later confirms they also recognize `session_id` / `x-session-affinity`, flipping the compat gate becomes a reasonable follow-up — this PR does not foreclose that path. ## Scope and caveats - This PR changes behavior only for the `zai` provider. No other provider is touched. - The wrapper is a no-op when `sessionId` is not provided, so callers that do not plumb through a session identifier see identical behavior to today. - Baseline data was captured on a deployment that served GLM-5.1 through an OpenAI-compatible proxy layer. The after-PR run is against `api.z.ai` directly using the bundled `zai` provider. Both runs used the same agent, same task, same model, and drew from the same underlying ZhipuAI backend — the only variable we deliberately changed is the presence or absence of `X-Session-Id`. - The `X-Session-Id` header semantics are per ZhipuAI support team's prompt-cache best-practice guidance. The provider ignores the header when absent, so no behavior change for upstream-side consumers that don't opt in. ## Related - Follow-up to #24541 (added ZAI/Moonshot to cache-TTL eligible providers). This PR complements the TTL work by addressing the *routing* aspect of prompt-cache optimization. - Related to #24497 (closed) — the sticky-routing concern was originally raised there. ## Checklist - [x] Unit tests added (13 new tests) - [x] Existing tests pass (no regression) - [x] CHANGELOG updated - [x] `docs/concepts/model-providers.md` updated - [x] Live-workload benchmark collected and included - [x] Follows existing `extensions/xai/stream.ts` pattern for consistency

target.title: feat(memory-lancedb): add smart extraction with atomic fact decomposition
target.body:
Add LLM-based memory extraction that decomposes conversations into atomic, self-contained memories via a single LLM call.

Key design (differs from Pro's multi-pipeline approach):
- Single-shot extraction instead of extract→classify→dedup→merge
- Atomic fact granularity (one memory = one information point)
- Third-person perspective enforced (not raw user text)
- Time resolution (relative → absolute dates)
- Language matching (Chinese input → Chinese output)
- Structured output with key/value/tags for retrieval enhancement
- Importance floor at 0.5 (below = not stored)

Prompt design draws from HonAgent (atomic facts, attribution), Memos (structured output, language matching, dedup), and Pro (category classification, importance scoring).

Config: smartExtraction.enabled + smartExtraction.model
Backward compatible: regex path used when not enabled.

target.title: fix: pass through image/audio/resource content blocks in MCP HTTP gateway
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes the HTTP MCP gateway stripping image, audio, and resource fields from tool results.

## Root Cause

The `normalizeToolCallContent` function was mapping all content blocks to only `{type, text}`, discarding other fields like `data`, `mimeType`, `resource`, `uri`, etc. This caused downstream Zod validation to fail for any tool returning non-text content.

## Fix

- Pass through valid content blocks directly to preserve all fields
- Only apply fallback transformation for strings or malformed blocks
- Updated type definition to include all MCP content block types

## Test Plan

- Added comprehensive test coverage for all content block types (text, image, audio, resource, resource_link)
- All 9 new tests pass
- Manually verified that image blocks now retain `data` and `mimeType` fields

Closes openclaw#68037

target.title: Control UI + auto-reply: strip leaked agent scaffolding and metadata
target.body:
## Summary

Three related bugs where Control UI (and any user-facing surface that shares `stripInboundMetadata`) leaks message internals that should already have been stripped:

1. `renderGroupedMessage` was extracting text directly from `normalizedMessage.content` instead of going through `extractText`/`processMessageText`, so `<think>`/`<final>` scaffolding from Gemini-family models and inbound metadata sentinels were rendered verbatim in the chat bubble.
2. `stripInboundMetadata` only handled the structured JSON-block sentinels. Per-line `System:` / `System (untrusted):` event prefixes emitted by `session-system-events.ts`, and the paired "An async command you ran earlier has completed." / "Current time: …" internal agent instructions, still leaked into user-visible text.
3. With (1) and (2) fixed, user turns whose text consisted entirely of now-stripped metadata still rendered an orphan "You · 12:34" bubble (avatar + sender label + timestamp + delete button, no content) because `renderMessageGroup` had no group-level suppression analogous to the per-message check inside `renderGroupedMessage`.

## Changes

- `ui/src/ui/chat/message-extract.ts`: export `processMessageText` so other renderers can share the same per-role strip contract.
- `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.ts`:
  - route `renderGroupedMessage`'s text extraction through `processMessageText` (assistant → `stripThinkingTags`, user → `stripInboundMetadata(stripEnvelope(...))`, other → `stripEnvelope`).
  - add a group-level emptiness precheck in `renderMessageGroup` so a group whose inner messages all render empty returns `nothing` instead of leaving an orphan avatar/footer behind.
- `src/auto-reply/reply/strip-inbound-meta.ts`:
  - add `SYSTEM_EVENT_LINE_RE` and an `INTERNAL_INSTRUCTION_LINE_PREFIXES` list.
  - extend the fast-path `SENTINEL_FAST_RE` to include `System:` / `System (untrusted):` and the instruction prefixes so these variants still trigger the slow parse.
  - in the main loop, drop lines matching either pattern. Only line-anchored matches are stripped so prose like "Operating System: Linux" is preserved.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test ui/src/ui/chat/message-extract.test.ts` — new `processMessageText` suite covers the empty-`<think></think>` + `<final>` variant I actually hit with `gemini-3-flash-preview`, plus the standard paired shape and plain pass-through for assistant/user roles.
- [x] `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/strip-inbound-meta.test.ts` — new `per-line system events and internal instructions` suite covers consecutive `System (untrusted):` exec lines, trusted `System:` lines, the async-command / current-time instruction pair, and guards against stripping the inline "Operating System: Linux" prose variant.
- [x] `pnpm tsgo`, `pnpm check`, `pnpm check:import-cycles`, `pnpm check:madge-import-cycles` — all green on the PR branch (run via `scripts/committer` pre-commit).
- [x] Manual smoke on a live self-hosted OpenClaw instance with Gemini 3 Flash Preview: `<final>…</final>` segments no longer render verbatim, `System (untrusted):` / async-command lines no longer surface in user bubbles, and orphan "You · HH:MM" empty groups no longer appear after metadata-only turns. Existing `<think>` reasoning blocks still surface correctly in the dedicated reasoning panel.

No config schema, plugin-SDK surface, or build output shape was changed.

target.title: feat(memory-core): add multi-strategy chunking support
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Memory search chunking was hardcoded to a single fixed-size strategy, offering no flexibility for different document types (markdown-heavy, narrative, multi-lingual, hierarchical) that benefit from different segmentation approaches.
- **Why it matters:** Poor chunking quality directly degrades memory search recall and precision. Different document structures require different splitting strategies — fixed-size token windows lose semantic boundaries, while heading-aware or sentence-aware splits preserve meaning at chunk borders.
- **What changed:**
  - Introduced a pluggable chunking strategy framework in `src/memory-host-sdk/host/chunking/` with 6 strategies: 
    - `fixed-size` (original, default)
    - `markdown-heading`
    - `sentence`
    - `semantic` (embedding-based cosine distance breakpoints)
    - `lumber` (LLM paragraph-level content-shift detection, per arXiv:2406.17526)
    - `hichunk` (hierarchical multi-level LLM-driven semantic segmentation, arXiv:2509.11552)
  - Added a `ChunkingStrategy` interface (`types.ts`), a factory function `resolveChunkingStrategy` (`resolve.ts`), and per-strategy implementations with configurable parameters and defaults.
  - Extended the config surface: `agents.defaults.memorySearch.chunking.strategy` and per-strategy fields (`tokens`, `overlap`, `maxDepth`, `maxTokens`, `targetTokens`, `overlapSentences`, `bufferSize`, `breakpointPercentileThreshold`, `theta`, `completionModel`, `windowSize`, `lineMaxLen`, `maxLevel`, `recurrentType`) in types (`types.tools.ts`), zod schema (`zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts`), and schema help (`schema.help.ts`).
  - Updated `resolveChunkingConfig` in `src/agents/memory-search.ts` to build strategy-specific config with defaults and clamping.
  - Updated `manager-embedding-ops.ts` to resolve the configured strategy at index time and route through `strategy.chunk()` instead of the old `chunkMarkdown()` directly.
  - Updated `manager-reindex-state.ts` with `chunkingConfigDiffers()` to detect per-strategy config changes and trigger full reindex when needed, with backward-compat for legacy `chunkTokens`/`chunkOverlap` meta.
  - Updated `manager-sync-ops.ts` to persist `chunkingConfig` in index meta alongside legacy `chunkTokens`/`chunkOverlap`.
  - Added comprehensive unit tests in `chunking.test.ts` covering all 6 strategies, helper functions, and the factory.
  - Added reindex-state tests for chunking config drift detection across strategies and legacy meta compat.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** The embedding pipeline, vector/FTS storage schema, query/search path, hybrid scoring, and session sync logic are untouched. The default behavior (`fixed-size` with existing defaults) is preserved for all existing users.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a new feature, not a bug fix.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — new feature, not a regression fix. New test coverage is included.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/memory-host-sdk/host/chunking/chunking.test.ts`, `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-reindex-state.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - Each strategy produces valid `MemoryChunk[]` with correct `startLine`/`endLine`/`text`/`hash`/`embeddingInput`
  - Factory resolves correct strategy and throws on missing dependencies (provider for semantic, completionFn for lumber/hichunk)
  - Reindex state correctly detects config drift per-strategy and handles legacy meta backward-compat
  - Helper functions (sentence splitting, heading parsing, LLM answer parsing, etc.) produce expected outputs
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Unit tests exercise each strategy's core splitting logic and the config resolution/reindex detection in isolation without requiring a running embedding provider or LLM.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing `manager-reindex-state.test.ts` was extended with chunking-specific cases.
- If no new test is added, why not: New tests **are** added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config key: `agents.defaults.memorySearch.chunking.strategy` — accepts `"fixed-size"` (default), `"markdown-heading"`, `"sentence"`, `"semantic"`, `"lumber"`, or `"hichunk"`.
- New strategy-specific config keys under `agents.defaults.memorySearch.chunking.*` (see schema help for documentation of each).
- `lumber` and `hichunk` strategies require `chunking.completionModel` to be set; config resolution throws if missing.
  - `lumber` using any general LLM as `chunking.completionModel`
  - `hichunk` needs to serve the model released by arXiv:2509.11552 as `chunking.completionModel`
- `semantic` strategy requires a working embedding provider (using `agents.defaults.memorySearch.model` as embedding provider).
- Changing the strategy or its parameters triggers a full memory reindex on next sync.
- Default behavior is **unchanged**: existing users who have not set `chunking.strategy` continue to use `fixed-size` with the same defaults (`tokens=400`, `overlap=80`).

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[memory file] -> chunkMarkdown(fixedSize) -> [MemoryChunk[]] -> embed + index

After:
[memory file] -> resolveChunkingStrategy(config) -> strategy.chunk(content) -> [MemoryChunk[]] -> embed + index
                        |
                        ├── fixed-size    (token window + overlap)
                        ├── markdown-heading (heading boundaries + fixed-size fallback)
                        ├── sentence      (sentence boundaries + token budget)
                        ├── semantic      (embedding cosine distance breakpoints)
                        ├── lumber        (LLM paragraph content-shift detection)
                        └── hichunk       (hierarchical multi-level LLM segmentation)
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No` (semantic/lumber/hichunk use existing embedding provider and completion model infrastructure)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / Bun
- Model/provider: Any embedding provider (OpenAI, local, etc.) + any completion model for lumber/hichunk
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted):
  ```json
  {
    "models": {
      "mode": "merge",
      "providers": {
        "sglang": {
          "baseUrl": "***",
          "apiKey": "***",
          "api": "openai-completions",
          "models": [
            {
              "id": "deepseek-v3.2",
              "name": "deepseek-v3.2",
              "reasoning": false,
              "input": [
                "text"
              ]
            }
          ]
        },
        "vllm": {
          "baseUrl": "***",
          "apiKey": "***",
          "api": "openai-completions",
          "models": [
            {
              "id": "Youtu-HiChunk",
              "name": "Youtu-HiChunk",
              "reasoning": false,
              "input": [
                "text"
              ]
            }
          ]
        }
      }
    },
    "agents": {
      "defaults": {
        "model": {
          "primary": "sglang/deepseek-v3.2"
        },
        "models": {
          "sglang/deepseek-v3.2": {},
          "vllm/Youtu-HiChunk": {}
        },
        "workspace": "/root/.openclaw/workspace",
        "memorySearch": {
          "enabled": true,
          "provider": "auto",
          "remote": {
            "baseUrl": "***",       // embeding model base url
            "apiKey": "***",        // embeding model api key
            "batch": {
              "enabled": true
            }
          },
          "model": "youtu-embedding-2B",
          "chunking": {
            "strategy": "markdown-heading",     // default "fixed-size"
            "tokens": 400,
            "overlap": 80,
            "maxTokens": 400,
            "maxDepth": 3,
            "targetTokens": 400,
            "overlapSentences": 1,
            "bufferSize": 1,
            "breakpointPercentileThreshold": 95,
            "theta": 550,
            "completionModel": "vllm/Youtu-HiChunk",    // set to "sglang/deepseek-v3.2" for lumber chunking strategy, and "vllm/Youtu-HiChunk" for hichunk strategy.
            "windowSize": 16384,
            "lineMaxLen": 100,
            "maxLevel": 2,
            "recurrentType": 2,
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Set `agents.defaults.memorySearch.chunking.strategy` to any of the 6 strategies (e.g. `"markdown-heading"`).
2. Add or update memory files in the workspace.
3. Run `openclaw memory index --force` or trigger memory indexing.

### Expected

- Memory files are chunked using the selected strategy.
- Chunks have correct `startLine`/`endLine` ranges.
- Changing strategy or strategy params triggers a full reindex on next sync.
- Omitting `chunking.strategy` uses `fixed-size` with original defaults (backward-compat).

### Actual

- (Verified as expected)

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

`chunking.test.ts` — 50+ test cases across all strategies and the factory pass. `manager-reindex-state.test.ts` — reindex detection tests pass for all strategy drift scenarios and legacy compat.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - All 6 strategies produce valid non-empty chunks for representative documents.
  - `fixed-size` output matches pre-refactor `chunkMarkdown` output (identical chunks).
  - `markdown-heading` correctly splits at h1/h2/h3 and sub-chunks oversized sections.
  - `sentence` respects token budget and overlap carry.
  - `semantic` calls `embedBatch` and splits on high cosine distance.
  - `lumber` and `hichunk` call the completion function and handle LLM errors gracefully.
  - Config resolution applies defaults and clamping per-strategy.
  - Reindex detection triggers on strategy change, param change, and legacy meta migration.
  - End-to-end sync + search with each strategy against a real SQLite index.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Empty content → `[]` for all strategies.
  - Single sentence/paragraph → single chunk (short-circuit path).
  - CJK-heavy text in fixed-size and sentence strategies.
  - Missing `completionModel` for lumber/hichunk → clear error message.
  - Missing embedding provider for `semantic` → clear error message.
  - Legacy meta without `chunkingConfig.strategy` → correct backward-compat comparison.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Performance impact of semantic strategy with large embedding batches.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — default strategy remains `fixed-size` with unchanged defaults; existing configs and index meta are fully supported.
- Config/env changes? `Yes` — new optional config keys under `agents.defaults.memorySearch.chunking.*`.
- Migration needed? `No` — existing indexes continue to work. Changing strategy triggers auto-reindex on next sync.
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: `N/A`

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `lumber` and `hichunk` strategies make LLM calls during indexing, which can be slow and costly for large memory directories.
  - Mitigation: These are opt-in strategies, not defaults. Both strategies handle LLM errors gracefully with fallback chunking. Token estimation uses local CJK-aware heuristics to minimize unnecessary LLM calls.
- Risk: Semantic strategy requires a working embedding provider at index time; provider outage would block indexing.
  - Mitigation: Factory throws a clear error if provider is missing. Users can fall back to non-provider strategies.
- Risk: Reindex detection for new strategies may produce false negatives on very old index meta formats.
  - Mitigation: `chunkingConfigDiffers` includes explicit legacy path that triggers reindex when switching from legacy meta to any non-fixed-size strategy.

target.title: fix(minimax): disable tool call ID sanitization for Anthropic-compatible API
target.body:
## Summary

Re-opens #65371 against the current replay-hook API (the original PR was written before the `buildProviderReplayFamilyHooks` factory landed and no longer applies).

- **Problem:** MiniMax's Anthropic-compatible API generates its own tool call IDs and rejects tool results if the echoed ID doesn't match exactly (error 2013: *"tool result's tool id not found"*).
- **Why it matters:** The default `hybrid-anthropic-openai` replay policy sets `sanitizeToolCallIds: true` + `toolCallIdMode: "strict"`, which rewrites IDs and breaks the MiniMax roundtrip. All tool use fails.
- **What changed:** Introduces a thin `MINIMAX_REPLAY_HOOKS` wrapper around `HYBRID_ANTHROPIC_OPENAI_REPLAY_HOOKS` that overrides `buildReplayPolicy` to set `sanitizeToolCallIds: false` when `modelApi === "anthropic-messages"`. `MINIMAX_PROVIDER_HOOKS` consumes the wrapper, so both the `minimax` and `minimax-portal` providers inherit it through a single spread.
- **What did NOT change:** OpenAI-compatible paths (`openai-completions`) keep strict sanitization. The factory API itself isn't touched (could be a follow-up to add a `sanitizeToolCallIds` knob to `buildProviderReplayFamilyHooks` so consumers don't need the wrapper pattern).

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Integrations (MiniMax provider plugin)

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #65380
- Closes #41257 — MiniMax models' tool calls are stripped instead of executed
- Supersedes #65371 (API drift)
- Related to #41839 — MiniMax M2.5 tool calls have no effect
- Same pattern as #34176 (ollama skip) and #52644 (Anthropic skip), scoped to MiniMax
- Prior attempt #24599 tried to fix error 2013 by adding more sanitization — the correct fix is to disable it

## Root Cause

- Root cause: the hybrid-anthropic-openai replay policy strictly sanitizes tool call IDs, but MiniMax's Anthropic-compatible API requires byte-exact ID echo.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no per-provider opt-out for tool-ID sanitization in the shared replay-family factory.
- Contributing context: fix follows the same pattern used for ollama and Anthropic, but scoped via a wrapper because the factory options don't yet expose a sanitizeToolCallIds knob.

## Regression Test Plan

- `extensions/minimax/index.test.ts` asserts `sanitizeToolCallIds: false` for the minimax provider on `anthropic-messages`, and `true` for the minimax-portal provider on `openai-completions`. Both cases covered.

## Verified locally

- `pnpm test extensions/minimax/index.test.ts` — 8/8 pass
- `pnpm build` clean
- End-to-end: a production MiniMax-M2.7 deployment running via this change executes tool calls (browser, web_search, exec) without error 2013.

target.title: Clear stale subagent lineage on top-level sessions
target.body:
Fixes #67943.

## Summary

Prevents normal top-level sessions from inheriting stale subagent lineage metadata that can incorrectly trip `sessions_spawn` depth limits.

## What changed

- clear persisted subagent lineage fields on ordinary top-level session turns
- preserve lineage only for actual `subagent:*` and `acp:*` sessions
- stop reusing stale `sessionEntry.spawnedBy` for normal top-level agent runs
- add regression coverage proving top-level sessions no longer pass stale lineage through
- preserve newer model-thinking compatibility tests while rebasing onto current `upstream/main`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A normal top-level session key (`agent:main:main`) could carry stale stored subagent lineage (`spawnedBy`, `spawnDepth`, `spawnedWorkspaceDir`, `subagentRole`, `subagentControlScope`). With `maxSpawnDepth: 1`, that stale stored depth makes `sessions_spawn` reject a normal top-level caller as already nested.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout for PR #67946 on Linux/Node 22, rebased onto current `origin/main`, head `a1cc7e7681ccdb488f9fa2bb1d78823995291d25`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a redacted local runtime proof from the patched checkout. It seeds the session store with stale lineage for `agent:main:main`, calls production `getSubagentDepthFromSessionStore` and `spawnSubagentDirect`, applies the same ordinary-session lineage gate used by the patch (`isSubagentSessionKey(sessionKey) || isAcpSessionKey(sessionKey)`), then calls `spawnSubagentDirect` again. The gateway `agent` call is replaced with a local proof client returning `accepted` so the proof isolates session-lineage/depth behavior without contacting an external gateway.
- **Evidence after fix:** Copied terminal output from the after-fix command:

  ```text
  proof head: a1cc7e7681ccdb488f9fa2bb1d78823995291d25
  session key: agent:main:main
  is subagent/acp: false false
  before cleanup entry: {"sessionId":"11111111-1111-4111-8111-111111111111","updatedAt":1778983631000,"sessionStartedAt":1778983631000,"lastInteractionAt":1778983631000,"spawnedBy":"agent:main:subagent:stale-parent","spawnDepth":1,"spawnedWorkspaceDir":"/stale/subagent/workspace","subagentRole":"leaf","subagentControlScope":"operator.admin"}
  before cleanup depth: 1
  before cleanup sessions_spawn status: forbidden
  before cleanup sessions_spawn error: sessions_spawn is not allowed at this depth (current depth: 1, max: 1)
  after cleanup entry: {}
  after cleanup depth: 0
  after cleanup sessions_spawn status: accepted
  after cleanup sessions_spawn runId: proof-run-1
  after cleanup sessions_spawn childSessionKey: agent:main:subagent:3ea9be1a-ca77-49e9-b9f5-cd08d6d1ffc4
  PASS: true
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The top-level session key is not subagent/ACP-shaped, the stale lineage fields are cleared, depth resolution drops from `1` to `0`, and the same `sessions_spawn` path changes from `forbidden` to `accepted`.
- **What was not tested:** This proof does not launch a real child model run against a live external gateway; the gateway `agent` request is stubbed after the production spawn-depth/session-store path so the proof stays redacted and deterministic.

## Validation

- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm run lint:plugins:no-extension-test-core-imports`
- `CI=true pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/agent-command.live-model-switch.test.ts`
  - `Test Files 1 passed (1)`
  - `Tests 20 passed (20)`
- `pnpm check:test-types`

Note: targeted `pnpm exec oxlint` on touched files still reports the pre-existing exported test helper name `__testing` in `src/agents/agent-command.ts`, so I did not count that as a passing validation gate.

target.title: fix(nextcloud-talk): parse rich object file shares from webhook payload
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #29152

When a user shares a file or image in Nextcloud Talk, the bot webhook receives the message but the NC Talk plugin was not parsing the rich object parameters — so the agent never saw the attachment.

NC Talk encodes file shares as rich object messages where `object.content` is a JSON string:
```json
{"message":"{file}","parameters":{"file":{"type":"file","id":"12345","name":"IMG_123.jpg","path":"Talk/IMG_123.jpg","link":"https://cloud.example.com/f/12345","mimetype":"image/jpeg","preview-available":"yes"}}}
```

Previously `payloadToInboundMessage()` passed this raw JSON string as `text`, leaving `mediaUrls` unpopulated.

## Changes

### `monitor.ts`
- Adds `parseRichObjectContent()` helper that detects JSON-encoded rich object payloads
- Extracts file links into `mediaUrls` (https/http only — rejects `file://`, `javascript:`, etc.)
- Resolves `{placeholder}` names for human-readable `displayText` (covers file shares, mentions, deck cards, polls, etc.)
- Uses `replaceAll` for placeholder substitution to handle duplicate occurrences
- Falls back to raw `content` for non-rich-object text — no behaviour change for regular messages

### `types.ts`
- Adds `mediaUrls?: string[]` to `NextcloudTalkInboundMessage`

### `inbound.ts`
- Passes `MediaUrls` into `finalizeInboundContext` (verified: already consumed natively as `string[]`)
- Relaxes the empty-body guard to allow media-only messages through (previously silently dropped)

## Tests

- `monitor.attachment.test.ts` — 7 unit tests: file share, text passthrough, malformed JSON fallback, name fallback, mention resolution, `file://` URL rejection, multi-file
- `monitor.live-integration.test.ts` — 5 integration tests using real HMAC-signed payloads against the webhook server

All 12 tests pass. TypeScript compiles clean (`tsc --noEmit`).

## Before / After

**Before:** Sharing a file in NC Talk → agent receives raw JSON string, never sees the attachment.

**After:** Sharing a file → agent receives `text = "filename.jpg"` and `mediaUrls = ["https://..."]` — can immediately process the image via vision without any manual workaround.

target.title: feat: add configurable timestamps to TUI chat messages
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #48708 — adds an option to display timestamps on each message in the TUI chat interface.

## Usage

Enable in config:
```json
{
  "tui": {
    "showTimestamps": true
  }
}
```

Messages then display as:
```
[11:32] system message here
[11:32]
user message here
[11:33] assistant response here
```

Disabled by default to preserve the current clean look.

## Changes

- `src/tui/components/chat-log.ts`: Added `showTimestamps` property and `formatTimestamp()` helper. `addUser`, `addSystem`, and `startAssistant` prepend `[HH:MM]` when enabled.
- `src/tui/tui.ts`: Read `tui.showTimestamps` from config and pass to ChatLog.

target.title: ACP: add gateway-owned node-backed runtime
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw did not yet have a complete **node-backed ACP runtime** where the gateway owns the durable state and a paired node acts only as the execution worker.
- Why it matters: without that split, restart/replay/reconnect behavior is too dependent on the live process, which makes lease fencing, terminal resolution, and outward delivery recovery harder to reason about and prove.
- What this PR adds:
  - a durable ACP gateway store for sessions, runs, accepted worker events, leases, terminal state, replay targets, and projector checkpoints
  - gateway ingestion of ACP worker events coming back from paired nodes
  - a real node-host ACP worker bridge and gateway/node command plumbing
  - a durable ACP reply projection/replay path so restart recovery can resume from gateway state instead of only from the live iterator
  - startup recovery for incomplete ACP node runs
  - restart/reconnect/cancel/close race hardening with focused restart/replay test coverage
  - architecture / protocol / verification docs for the new backend
- Landing-only follow-up: after rebasing onto current `main`, this PR also includes a few **test-only** isolation fixes in Feishu / Google / Google Chat extension tests that were needed to make the landing branch green. Those are not ACP runtime behavior changes.

## What changed by area

### 1) Durable ACP gateway state

New ACP store/state plumbing under `src/acp/store/**`:

- persists ACP sessions and runs
- persists accepted worker events
- tracks leases / lease epochs
- resolves canonical terminal state on the gateway
- stores replay targets and projector checkpoints separately from the runtime cursor

This is the foundation that lets the gateway stay authoritative even when execution happens on a paired node.

### 2) Gateway ↔ node ACP worker path

Main implementation areas:

- `src/gateway/**`
- `src/infra/node-commands.ts`
- `src/node-host/**`

This adds the node-backed ACP execution path:

- gateway can start/control ACP work on a paired node
- node-host can run the ACP worker role
- worker events stream back to the gateway over the existing node transport
- gateway remains the source of truth for run state and terminal resolution

### 3) Durable reply projection and replay

Main implementation areas:

- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-replay.ts`

This PR moves ACP outward delivery onto a durable replayable path:

- reply targets are persisted per run
- projector checkpoints are advanced only after successful delivery
- restart recovery can replay the unfinished suffix from durable state
- reconnect and duplicate accepted events do not re-deliver already completed outward effects

### 4) Startup recovery / reconnect handling

Main implementation areas:

- `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-node-events.ts`
- `src/acp/runtime/acp-node.ts`

This adds the gateway-side recovery flow needed to:

- resume incomplete projection after restart
- keep reconnect handling deterministic
- prevent stale-worker wins
- preserve canonical terminal behavior across restart/replay windows

### 5) Test harnesses and regression coverage

Main test additions/expansions:

- `src/acp/store/store.restart.test.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-node-events.acp.test.ts`
- `src/node-host/invoke-acp.test.ts`
- `src/acp/test-harness/restart-harness.ts`

These cover the core failure windows this backend needs to survive:

- restart after accepted events but before replay checkpoint advancement
- restart after terminal persistence but before final outward convergence
- reconnect replay of already accepted events
- cancel / complete / close race handling
- restart recovery with a real memory cut instead of reusing in-process objects

### 6) Docs included in this PR

Main docs:

- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-protocol.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-verification.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-runtime-program.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice4-summary.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice4-final-judge.md`

These are included because this was developed as a staged architecture / verification effort and the final accepted behavior is documented there.

## Explicit scope boundaries / v1 limitations

This PR does **not**:

- turn nodes into mini-gateways
- add automatic cross-node continuation for an in-flight run
- claim durable resumed tool-message edit support after restart

Current conservative post-restart tool-update behavior remains:

- `append_only_after_restart`

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: ACP node runtime architecture / protocol / verification docs under `docs/experiments/plans/`
- Related: landing-only extension test isolation fixes after rebase onto current `main`

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- OpenClaw now has a first-class ACP node-backed runtime path where the gateway owns durable ACP truth and paired nodes execute the worker role.
- Gateway restart/recovery can resume incomplete ACP outward projection from durable state.
- Duplicate accepted worker-event replay on reconnect is hardened so it does not duplicate outward delivery.
- Post-restart tool updates intentionally follow the documented conservative v1 rule: `append_only_after_restart`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes
- Data access scope changed? Yes

### Security / trust-boundary notes

- Paired nodes can now advertise ACP worker capability and receive ACP runtime commands over the existing gateway↔node trust boundary.
- Gateway↔node websocket traffic now carries ACP worker control / event flows in addition to existing node commands.
- Gateway now persists ACP runtime state needed for durable replay/recovery.
- The design mitigates stale-worker / duplicate-delivery risks via lease epochs, canonical terminal resolution, durable replay checkpoints, and reconnect/replay handling on the gateway.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (landing worktree on vm1) + GitHub Actions CI
- Runtime/container: Node / pnpm; Bun build path also checked
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): ACP gateway↔node runtime path
- Relevant config (redacted): ACP runtime restart/replay harnesses and paired-node fixtures

### Verified locally

- ACP targeted verification gate:
  - `pnpm test -- src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts src/acp/store/store.test.ts src/acp/store/store.restart.test.ts src/acp/store/gateway-events.test.ts src/gateway/server-node-events.acp.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-acp.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.acp.test.ts`
- TypeScript guardrail:
  - `pnpm tsgo`
- Landing-only extension test fixes:
  - `pnpm test:extensions`

### CI / build notes

- `pnpm build` still fails only with the pre-existing Bun wrapper issue:
  - `Cannot find module './cjs/index.cjs' from ''`
- That build failure was already present and is not introduced by the ACP runtime changes in this PR.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the ACP-focused local gate listed above.
- Verified `pnpm tsgo` passes.
- Verified the rebased landing branch’s extension test-isolation fixes with local `pnpm test:extensions`.
- Verified the PR now includes the architecture / protocol / verification / final-judge docs for this backend.

What I did **not** verify:

- I did **not** get a clean `pnpm build` because of the pre-existing Bun wrapper failure noted above.
- I did **not** run a fresh manual end-to-end Discord/real-node interactive repro outside the checked-in restart/replay and node-host harnesses.

## Review Guide

If you want the shortest path through this PR, review in this order:

1. **Core architecture / intent**
   - `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime.md`
   - `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-protocol.md`
2. **Gateway-owned durable state**
   - `src/acp/store/**`
3. **Node execution bridge**
   - `src/node-host/**`
   - `src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.ts`
   - `src/gateway/server-node-events.ts`
4. **Durable outward replay path**
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-replay.ts`
5. **Recovery and proofs**
   - `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts`
   - `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts`
6. **Ignore as landing-only test fixes unless you are checking CI fallout**
   - `extensions/feishu/**`
   - `extensions/google/**`
   - `extensions/googlechat/**`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No explicit migration required
- Migration needed? No

Notes:

- This adds a new ACP runtime/backend path; it does not force existing deployments to switch to it immediately.
- Post-restart tool updates intentionally remain append-only in v1.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR or avoid selecting/using the `acp-node` runtime path until fixed.
- Files/config to restore: ACP runtime/store/gateway/node-host changes under `src/acp/**`, `src/node-host/**`, `src/gateway/**`, plus the accompanying docs if reverting wholesale.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: duplicate final delivery after restart, stale worker winning after reconnect, projector checkpoint drift, or replay targeting the wrong route.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: restart/replay correctness is subtle in any durable projection system.
  - Mitigation: this PR includes restart harnesses, startup recovery tests, reconnect/replay ordering tests, and explicit protocol/verification docs.
- Risk: the PR is large and spans multiple ACP runtime layers.
  - Mitigation: the review guide above splits it into durable store, node bridge, replay path, recovery path, and landing-only CI fixes.
- Risk: paired-node ACP execution expands the gateway↔node command surface.
  - Mitigation: the gateway remains authoritative for leases, terminal resolution, checkpoints, and replay instead of trusting node-local completion.

---

## AI Checklist

- [x] **AI-assisted** — agent-harness / Codex driven implementation, review, and landing follow-ups
- [x] **Testing degree** — focused local verification plus CI
- [x] **Understanding confirmed** — this PR adds the gateway-owned ACP node-backed runtime and includes a few landing-only extension test fixes after rebase
- [ ] Prompts/session logs — available on request
