target.title: feat(matrix): opt-in mention bypass for in-thread replies (threadBindings.bypassMentionInBoundThreads)
target.body:
Fixes #85111.

## Summary

Adds an opt-in `channels.matrix.threadBindings.bypassMentionInBoundThreads` (boolean, default false). When true, the matrix inbound handler skips the room-level `requireMention` gate for events that are replying inside a thread the bot already has prior activity in.

This enables the common bot UX of "mention to open a thread, free-form conversation continues inside it" on `requireMention` rooms — currently impossible because every in-thread reply that omits the mention is dropped as `skipping room message: no-mention`.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: The matrix plugin's inbound handler drops in-thread replies in `requireMention: true` rooms even when the bot has been actively conversing in that thread. Users have to `@bot` on every line inside threads the bot opened, breaking the natural "mention to start, free-form continuation" UX. With this PR's opt-in flag, in-thread replies bypass the mention gate when the bot has a recorded session for the thread (via `recordInboundSession`'s thread-suffixed key) OR an explicit runtime binding (via `/focus`, `/acp bind`, etc.). Top-level messages keep `requireMention` enforcement; rooms without the flag set behave byte-identically to current `main`.

- **Real environment tested**: Unraid 7.2.5 host, `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:latest` (2026.5.19), Synapse via `ghcr.io/junkerderprovinz/matrix:latest` (AIO image), both containers on the same Docker bridge, Synapse Tailscale-served as `matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net`. Test room `parents` (`!pgXEKemZcQTfeiaNvM:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net`) configured under `channels.matrix.groups[<roomId>] = { requireMention: true, skills: [actual-budget, diagram-maker, news-summary] }`, three members (`@paulmeier`, `@openclaw`, `@admin`), E2EE on, `threadReplies: \"always\"`, `threadBindings: { enabled: true, idleHours: 24, spawnSessions: true, defaultSpawnContext: \"fork\" }`. The patched matrix monitor bundle (`/root/.openclaw/extensions/matrix/dist/monitor-4YYdKe_C.js`) was overlaid onto the running container via `docker cp`, then OpenClaw restarted. The unpatched bundle was preserved at `monitor-4YYdKe_C.js.bak` for revert.

- **Exact steps or command run after the patch**: Live-environment verification — overlay the patched matrix monitor bundle onto the running container, restart, then exercise the in-thread reply path in `#parents`:

  ```
  $ ssh root@<unraid> 'docker cp OpenClaw:/root/.openclaw/extensions/matrix/dist/monitor-4YYdKe_C.js /tmp/monitor-orig.js'
  $ scp root@<unraid>:/tmp/monitor-orig.js /tmp/monitor-orig.js
  $ python3 -c \"src=open('/tmp/monitor-orig.js').read();
    old='const shouldBypassMention = allowTextCommands && isRoom && shouldRequireMention && !wasMentioned && !hasExplicitMention && commandAuthorized && hasControlCommandInMessage;';
    new='const inThreadReply = threadRootId !== undefined && threadRootId !== messageId; let threadSessionExists = false; if (inThreadReply) { try { const _storePath = core.channel.session.resolveStorePath(cfg.session?.store, { agentId: _route.agentId }); const _ts = core.channel.session.readSessionUpdatedAt({ storePath: _storePath, sessionKey: _route.sessionKey }); threadSessionExists = _ts !== undefined && _ts !== null; } catch (_e) { threadSessionExists = false; } } const inExistingBoundThread = inThreadReply && (threadSessionExists || _runtimeBindingId !== null); const shouldBypassMention = isRoom && shouldRequireMention && !wasMentioned && !hasExplicitMention && ((allowTextCommands && commandAuthorized && hasControlCommandInMessage) || inExistingBoundThread);';
    open('/tmp/monitor-patched.js','w').write(src.replace(old, new, 1))\"
  $ scp /tmp/monitor-patched.js root@<unraid>:/tmp/
  $ ssh root@<unraid> 'docker cp /tmp/monitor-patched.js OpenClaw:/root/.openclaw/extensions/matrix/dist/monitor-4YYdKe_C.js && docker restart OpenClaw'
  ```

  In `#parents`, posted `@openclaw ping` (top-level mention; opens a thread), then in that thread posted `hello there` (no mention).

  Runtime acceptance-criteria suite — runtime executed locally against the patched branch (`308da8c5`):

  ```
  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \\
      extensions/matrix/src/matrix/direct-room.test.ts \\
      extensions/matrix/src/matrix/direct-management.test.ts \\
      extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/direct.test.ts \\
      extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.test.ts \\
      extensions/matrix/src/matrix/send/targets.test.ts
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**: Live patched-runtime gateway log on the deployment, redacted — bot processing the in-thread reply that was previously dropped. After the user's mention of \`@openclaw\` opens the thread, the in-thread \`hello there\` follow-up triggers an outbound encrypted-message burst (Olm session prepares for the typing indicator + final reply) in the parents room. No \`skipping room message\` entries fire for the in-thread events:

  ```
  \"time\":\"2026-05-22T05:43:08.376-05:00\" \"matrix: logged in as @openclaw:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net\"
  \"time\":\"2026-05-22T05:44:08.376-05:00\" \"[MatrixClient.[!pgXEKemZcQTfeiaNvM:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net encryption]] m1779446648376.1: Checking for missing Olm sessions\"
  \"time\":\"2026-05-22T05:44:08.464-05:00\" \"[MatrixClient.[!pgXEKemZcQTfeiaNvM:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net encryption]] m1779446648464.2: Checking for missing Olm sessions\"
  \"time\":\"2026-05-22T05:44:08.537-05:00\" \"[MatrixClient.[!pgXEKemZcQTfeiaNvM:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net encryption]] m1779446648537.3: Checking for missing Olm sessions\"
  \"time\":\"2026-05-22T05:44:08.614-05:00\" \"[MatrixClient.[!pgXEKemZcQTfeiaNvM:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net encryption]] m1779446648614.4: Checking for missing Olm sessions\"
   (+ 7 more bursts of streaming-quiet typing updates ending at m1779446649228.11)
  ```

  11 outbound encrypted-message preparations in the same thread within 853 ms — the streaming-quiet typing-indicator pattern fires throughout the agent turn, then a final reply. Compare to the pre-patch deployment captured earlier in #85111's reproduction, where the same in-thread message produced a `\"message\":\"skipping room message\"` entry at `handler.ts:1032` with no outbound activity.

  Runtime acceptance suite (verbose) against `308da8c5` — the three behaviors this PR is responsible for:

  ```
   ✓ monitor/handler > createMatrixRoomMessageHandler > bypasses requireMention for in-thread replies when a session record already exists for the thread
   ✓ monitor/handler > createMatrixRoomMessageHandler > bypasses requireMention for in-thread replies when an explicit runtime binding exists (e.g. /focus)
   ✓ monitor/handler > createMatrixRoomMessageHandler > still drops in-thread replies without mention when bypassMentionInBoundThreads is off (default)
     (+ 107 other existing handler behaviors, all green)

   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  110 passed (110)
     Duration  2.92s
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: On the live patched deployment, an in-thread `hello there` (no `@openclaw` mention) inside the thread the bot just opened triggers the agent — Element shows `OpenClaw is typing…`, the user's message gets the `👀` ack reaction, the bot replies inside the same thread. Pre-patch, the identical message was logged as `skipping room message` and never reached the agent. Top-level non-mention messages in the same room continue to be dropped (legacy `requireMention` behavior preserved). With the new flag off (the default for everyone who doesn't opt in), behavior is byte-identical to current `main`, and the existing regression at `handler.test.ts:1617` (`does not refresh bound Matrix thread bindings for room messages dropped before routing`) continues to pass without modification.

- **What was not tested**: The unmodified-image runtime (i.e. with `bypassMentionInBoundThreads: true` set in `openclaw.json` and a CI-rebuilt `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:latest` carrying the patch through the official build pipeline). The live proof above uses an in-place bundle overlay on a running container, which exercises the patched mention-gate code path correctly but uses the always-on form of the discriminator (the running runtime has no way to read the new opt-in config field). The flag-gated form is what the source PR ships; the gate evaluation is covered by the three handler tests above, two of which exercise the bypass path and one of which exercises the legacy-default-off path. Maintainer-side CI rebuild will produce the final runtime artifact that exercises the flag-gated form end-to-end.

## Discriminator update vs the originally-pushed version

The first commit on this branch (`6f2a5b6e`) gated the bypass on `_runtimeBindingId !== null` only. Live testing surfaced that natural per-thread continuation does NOT produce a runtime binding — `sessionBindingService.resolveByConversation` returns `null` for threads that were opened by the bot replying, only for threads that have an explicit ACP/user-managed binding (via `/focus`, `/acp bind`, etc.). The handler tests in `6f2a5b6e` mocked `resolveByConversation` to return a binding, which masked the wrong discriminator: tests passed because the mock matched the broken code path, not because the real-world code path worked.

The current commit (`308da8c5`) replaces the single-signal check with an OR over two signals:

1. **Session-store record exists for the thread** — via `core.channel.session.readSessionUpdatedAt({ storePath, sessionKey: _route.sessionKey })`. The thread session key is set by `resolveMatrixInboundRoute` whenever `thread.threadId` is populated (always under `threadReplies: \"inbound\"`/`\"always\"`), and `recordInboundSession` persists it on the first message the bot processes in the thread. So this is the natural-continuation path.
2. **Explicit runtime binding exists** — the original `_runtimeBindingId !== null` check, kept as a parallel signal for the `/focus`-bound case.

The session-store path is the dominant case in real deployments; the runtime-binding path covers explicit-binding flows for parity. Either signal is sufficient.

## Files changed

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `extensions/matrix/src/config-schema.ts` | New `bypassMentionInBoundThreads` field in `matrixThreadBindingsSchema` |
| `extensions/matrix/src/types.ts` | Same field added to `MatrixThreadBindingsConfig` TS type |
| `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/index.ts` | Read `accountConfig.threadBindings?.bypassMentionInBoundThreads === true` and pass into handler params |
| `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.ts` | Handler params accept the flag; in-thread-reply discriminator computes session-store existence via `readSessionUpdatedAt`; OR with `_runtimeBindingId` and feeds `shouldBypassMention` |
| `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.test.ts` | Three tests: session-record bypass, runtime-binding bypass, legacy default-off drop |
| `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.test-helpers.ts` | Test harness option `bypassMentionInBoundThreads` |
| `docs/channels/matrix.md` | Documents the new field plus discriminators and the preserved legacy contract |

## Related

- #85017 / #85018 — DM classification veto on `is_direct: false` (companion PR; same `#parents`/`#adults` deployment thread).
- #85111 — the filed issue with full reproduction.

target.title: Fix memory_search wiki fallback and plugin tool registry reuse
target.body:
## Summary
- add a direct memory-wiki fallback for `memory_search` and `memory_get` when wiki corpus supplements are missing at runtime
- avoid mutating global plugin memory state during plugin tool resolution
- add regression coverage for wiki fallback and gateway-bindable registry reuse

## Problem
`memory_search(corpus="wiki")` could return no results even while `wiki_search` worked, because scoped activating plugin loads could wipe process-global memory wiki supplement registrations. Tool resolution could also over-reuse a gateway-bindable active registry that was not actually compatible with the requested plugin set.

## Fix
- add a direct `memory-wiki` fallback inside `memory-core`
- change plugin tool resolution to reuse only compatible active registries, otherwise perform a non-activating snapshot load

## Validation
- targeted Vitest coverage for memory-core wiki fallback
- targeted Vitest coverage for plugin tool registry reuse

target.title: Memory: validate dreaming cron frequency
target.body:
## Summary
- Validate `dreaming.frequency` up front so an invalid cron expression no longer fails silently later in the managed cron system.
- Adds `validateMemoryDreamingFrequency()` to the memory host SDK; returns a discriminated `{ valid }` result with a `reason: "inline-timezone" | "parse" | "timezone"` so callers can tailor guidance.
- Validator explicitly checks the IANA timezone via `Intl.DateTimeFormat` and forces `cron.nextRun(new Date())` after construction to defeat Croner's lazy-construction short-circuit.
- `extensions/memory-core` calls the validator before creating/updating the managed dreaming cron job. On invalid input it logs an error and returns `{ status: "invalid", removed: 0 }`, intentionally leaving the last-known-good managed job untouched so dreaming keeps running until the config is fixed.
- Inline `TZ=`/`CRON_TZ=` expressions get a targeted hint: `Timezone must be set via dreaming.timezone, not inline in the expression.` Plain parse errors do not.

## Test plan
- [x] `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/memory-host-sdk/dreaming.ts src/memory-host-sdk/dreaming.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.ts extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.test.ts`
- [x] `corepack pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- [x] `corepack pnpm test src/memory-host-sdk/dreaming.test.ts`
- [x] `corepack pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.test.ts`
- New tests cover: valid/invalid frequency validation, inline-timezone reason, invalid IANA timezone, plain-parse error does not surface timezone guidance, pre-existing managed cron job is preserved when the new config is invalid.

target.title: feat(memory-core): Memory v2 foundation — sidecar, ingest, rerank (all default-off)
target.body:
Additive foundation for a SQLite-backed Memory v2 sidecar and two pipelines
(ingest, rerank). Every surface is wired behind a plugin-config flag and
defaults to **off**, so this PR is behaviorally a no-op until a workspace
opts in via `memoryV2.ingest.enabled` / `memoryV2.rerank.enabled`.

## Why

Memory v2 aims to let `memory-core` track per-location signals (salience,
pinned/superseded status, last-access recency) alongside the existing
markdown files, and rerank `memory_search` results with those signals. This
PR lands the foundation without changing existing behavior.

## Slices (6 commits, intentionally atomic)

1. **Slice 0 — Sidecar foundation** (`2523e5a7`)
   `memory/v2-sidecar.db` schema + repo + `openSidecarDatabase`. No
   consumers yet.

2. **Slice 1a — Ingest internals** (`d1e9f3b0`)
   Dedupe, extract, handler, normalize, secret-filter, synthetic-ref.
   Pure module; not registered.

3. **Slice 1b — Ingest wiring** (`eb3d250e`)
   `registerMemoryV2Ingest(api)` subscribes to `agent_end`. Reads
   `memoryV2.ingest.enabled` via a type-guarded config check; returns a
   noop subscription when the flag is off.

4. **Slice 1.5 — Location keys + shadow-touch helper** (`1d33d0ad`)
   Canonical `memoryLocationId` / path normalization, `recordTouchedLocations`
   helper. Prerequisite for both rerank lookup and future shadow-row writes.

5. **Slice 2a — Rerank module** (`a1319c00`)
   `buildRerankWrapper`, `applyRerank`, `loadSidecarSignalsByLocations`.
   Pure module; not wired.

6. **Slice 2b — Rerank wiring** (`1e4431b6`)
   `createMemorySearchTool` gains an optional `rerank?: RerankFn`. When the
   plugin config enables rerank, `buildMemoryV2Rerank(api)` returns a
   `RerankFn` that is threaded into the tool factory. When off, the rerank
   field isn't even spread into the options bag — the call path is
   byte-identical to before.

## Safety properties

- **Default off**, enforced by explicit `=== true` check in both wirings.
  Truthy coercion (`1`, `"true"`) does not enable.
- **Type-guarded config reads** — malformed `memoryV2.*` never throws;
  it silently degrades to disabled.
- **Identity fallback on any rerank error** — `buildRerankWrapper` wraps
  its sidecar db calls in `try/catch` and returns original results on
  failure. A broken sidecar cannot break `memory_search`.
- **Per-workspace db cache** inside the wrapper closure; one `DatabaseSync`
  per `workspaceDir` per plugin activation.
- **Additive manifest** — `memoryV2.rerank` is a new object under the
  sibling `memoryV2.ingest` block added in Slice 1b; no existing keys
  changed.

## Upstream / update-survival notes

The hunk most likely to cause future rebase friction is the 13-line
pre-sort block in `extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts` (the `merged`/
`reranked` intermediate between result-merge and `.toSorted(...)`). That
area of the file has seen active upstream work (#67277, #65068). The hunk
is additive and flag-gated, so a conflict resolution is mechanical:
re-inject the `merged` intermediate and the conditional `reranked` call.

The rest of the surface is either:
- Low-churn (`openclaw.plugin.json` interior addition, `tools.test.ts`
  end-of-file append), or
- Contained in new files under `src/memory-v2/` that have no upstream
  counterpart.

## Known follow-ups (explicitly out of scope here)

- Seam hardening for `api.pluginConfig: unknown`, which is declared as a
  local type in both `ingest-wiring.ts` and `rerank-wiring.ts`. If the
  SDK renames the field, both wirings silently see `undefined` and stay
  disabled. A future small PR can replace the local type with the real
  SDK register-API type.
- "Shadow-only" mode: currently `shadowOnRecall` only runs when rerank
  itself is enabled. Collecting training signal before flipping rerank
  on would need a separate path.
- Cosmetic: `tools.ts:304` uses `merged as never` to bridge the union
  element type to `RerankableResult`. `as readonly RerankableResult[]`
  would be clearer. Not included to keep this PR scoped to feature work.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts` → 62 files / 643 passed / 3 skipped, 0 failed
- [x] `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/memory-core/tsconfig.json --noEmit` → clean
- [x] `pnpm exec oxlint` on all touched files → 0 warnings, 0 errors
- [x] `pnpm check` (full repo gate: no-conflict-markers, tool-display, host-env-policy:swift, import-cycles, madge-import-cycles, tsgo, extension-package-boundary, lint, webhook/auth lints) → all 11 gates clean

target.title: fix(mattermost): support filePath and path as mediaUrl fallbacks in handleAction
target.body:
## Summary

The Mattermost `handleAction` only checked `params.media` for media URL, ignoring `filePath` and `path` which are commonly used by agents. This is inconsistent with other channel handlers like Discord and Google Chat.

## Problem

When an agent uses the message tool with `filePath` or `path` instead of `media`:
- Discord handler: supports `media` → `path` → `filePath` fallback
- Google Chat handler: supports `media` → `filePath` → `path` fallback  
- Mattermost handler: only checks `media` → file upload fails silently

## Fix

Add fallback chain for media URL resolution:
1. `params.media`
2. `params.filePath`
3. `params.path`

## Related

This is a partial fix for #67784. The other parts (threadId and mediaLocalRoots) are already addressed by:
- PR #52120 (thread context inheritance)
- PR #58439 (mediaLocalRoots forwarding)

## Test Plan
- [x] Existing unit tests pass
- [x] Lint passes

Closes openclaw#67784 (filePath/path fallback only)

target.title: fix(feishu): target typing reaction on inbound
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: In Feishu groups with Topic mode (or `replyInThread`) enabled, the typing-indicator emoji reaction landed on the topic/thread root message instead of the user's inbound message. - Why it matters: User-visible confusion — the reaction did not correspond to the message being processed. - What changed: Threaded `inboundMessageId` (`ctx.messageId`) through `handleFeishuMessage` into `createFeishuReplyDispatcher`; `addTypingIndicator` now uses `inboundMessageId ?? replyToMessageId` so the reaction targets the user's actual message, with safe fallback. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Reply target, thread routing, outbound message metadata, non-group DM flows, webhook mode. Currently when user reply in a thread the typing reaction is applied on the main thread message Changed it to apply the typing react on the message that user sent int he thread ## Real behavior proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** In a Feishu group with Topic mode enabled, the bot's Typing emoji reaction landed on the topic / thread root message instead of the message the user just sent inside the topic. - **Real environment tested:** Live Feishu (Lark) group with Topic mode enabled, running the patched OpenClaw CLI against a real Feishu app over the WebSocket transport. Host: macOS 15, Node 22, pnpm 10.32.1. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** 1. Built the patched branch locally and started OpenClaw against the real Feishu app: `pnpm openclaw channels feishu run`. 2. Inside an existing topic in a Feishu group, @mentioned the bot with a new message. 3. Watched the bot's Typing emoji reaction land in the Lark UI on the message that had just been sent. - **Evidence after fix:** Lark UI screenshot taken on the same group after applying this patch — the Typing emoji reaction now attaches to the user's just-sent message inside the topic instead of the topic root. ![After fix — Typing reaction targets the user's inbound message inside the topic](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9ebce304-e8f0-4b09-a7cb-2e58ad3f1ac3) For comparison, before the fix the reaction landed on the thread root: ![Before fix — Typing reaction lands on the topic root](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/94dbdceb-475f-4d2a-a9c9-8148cc9783bb) - **Observed result after fix:** The Typing emoji reaction is rendered on the message the user just sent inside the topic. The topic / thread root no longer receives the keepalive reaction. Behavior in 1:1 DMs and non-topic groups is unchanged. - **What was not tested:** Webhook delivery mode (only the WebSocket transport was exercised live). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - N/A - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause - Root cause: In `createFeishuReplyDispatcher`, the typing reaction used `replyToMessageId` directly. In `replyInThread` / Topic mode, `replyToMessageId` points at the thread root, not the user's inbound message, so the reaction landed on the wrong message. - Missing detection / guardrail: No test covered typing-reaction target selection in topic / `replyInThread` modes. - Contributing context: The dispatcher was originally written for 1:1 DMs and non-topic groups where `replyToMessageId` coincides with the inbound message id. ## Regression Test Plan - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: when `inboundMessageId` is supplied, `addTypingIndicator` receives that id; when omitted, it falls back to `replyToMessageId`. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is purely in dispatcher input routing — a unit test over the dispatcher boundary is sufficient. - Existing test that already covers this: None before this PR. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A (tests added). ## User-visible / Behavior Changes The typing emoji reaction now lands on the message the user actually sent in topic / `replyInThread` modes. No behavior change for 1:1 DMs or non-topic groups. ## Diagram ```text Before (topic mode): user sends msg M in topic T → bot reacts Typing on T (thread root) After (topic mode): user sends msg M in topic T → bot reacts Typing on M (user's message) Fallback (DM / non-topic group): inboundMessageId === replyToMessageId → reaction still lands on user's message (unchanged) ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS 15 - Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm 10.32.1 - Model/provider: N/A (channel-layer fix) - Integration/channel: Feishu / Lark (WebSocket) - Relevant config: `channels.feishu.replyInThread=true` (or group Topic mode enabled) ### Steps 1. Configure a Feishu channel with `replyInThread` enabled (or use a group with Topic mode turned on). 2. @mention the bot inside an existing topic / thread and send a message. 3. Observe the typing emoji reaction in the Lark UI. ### Expected - Reaction lands on the message the user just sent. ### Actual (before fix) - Reaction lands on the topic / thread root. ## Evidence - [x] Passing test after fix (`extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts` covers both the `inboundMessageId` path and the fallback) - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: topic / thread reply, non-topic group, 1:1 DM - Edge cases checked: `inboundMessageId` omitted (fallback to `replyToMessageId`) - What I did **not** verify: webhook mode (only WebSocket tested) ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes (additive optional parameter with safe fallback) - Config/env changes? No - Migration needed? No ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: None identified — additive optional parameter; existing call paths remain correct via the `?? replyToMessageId` fallback. ---

target.title: fix: remove truncated preview from inbound system events
target.body:
## Summary

Removes the truncated 160-char message preview from system event headers for Slack, MS Teams, and Mattermost channels.

## Problem

System events were including a truncated preview of the message body:

```
System: [2026-04-15 21:56:40 MDT] Slack DM from Jared: What I'm saying is that you are can handle this task (you just showed me you can), but starting tomorrow (and including tomorrow), When we're planning the day o
```

This caused the model to sometimes reference the truncated header instead of the full message body, leading to responses like "your message got cut off at 'day o'" when the full message was delivered correctly.

## Solution

System events now only contain the notification label without message content:

```
System: [2026-04-15 21:56:40 MDT] Slack DM from Jared
```

The system event's purpose is notification ("a message arrived"), not content delivery. The full message body is delivered separately in the user turn.

## Changes

- `extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.ts` — remove preview from system event (keep for debug logging/type compat)
- `extensions/msteams/src/monitor-handler/message-handler.ts` — remove preview from system event (keep for debug logging)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` — remove preview from system event

## Testing

- `pnpm test:extension slack` — ✅ 641 tests passed
- `pnpm test:extension msteams` — ✅ 862 tests passed
- `pnpm test:extension mattermost` — ✅ 316 passed (1 timeout failure unrelated to changes)
- `pnpm build` — ✅
- Pre-commit hooks — ✅

Fixes #67503

target.title: test(anthropic): pin Opus 4.7 variant resolution + thinking-default regression coverage
target.body:
AI-assisted: yes (Claude). Testing level: focused tests.

## Summary

Pins the Opus 4.7 behavior that fixed #67710 so future refactors don't silently regress it. Test-only; no production code change.

Closes #67710.

### Why the issue is still open

The original "configured+missing" Opus 4.7 symptom was resolved by Peter's `628b454eff feat: default Anthropic to Opus 4.7` (~26 minutes after the issue was filed). The follow-up `a2753e2d9f fix: keep Opus 4.7 effort separate from adaptive thinking` pinned the thinking-level decision. Neither PR linked a `Closes #67710`, so the issue stayed open despite the behavior being live on `main`.

### Changes

`extensions/anthropic/index.test.ts` — new `describe("Opus 4.7 variant regression coverage")` block:

- `claude-opus-4.7` and `claude-opus-4-7-20260901` resolve from the 4.6 template family (exercises the dot and date-suffix branches of `resolveAnthropicForwardCompatModel`).
- `claude-opus-4-7` is marked as a modern model (regression coverage for `ANTHROPIC_MODERN_MODEL_PREFIXES`).
- `claude-opus-4.7` and `claude-opus-4-7-20260901` keep default thinking level `off` with `xhigh`/`adaptive`/`max` levels available — pins `a2753e2d9f`.

### Notes

- Earlier scope of this PR (adding Sonnet 4.7 forward-compat + Opus 4.7 adaptive-default) was dropped: Anthropic has not shipped Sonnet 4.7, and the Opus 4.7 adaptive-default change directly conflicted with `a2753e2d9f`.
- PDF tool test update from the earlier scope was also dropped — already on main via `892baf2e81`.

## Verification

- `pnpm test extensions/anthropic/index.test.ts` — 17/17 green, including 5 new cases.

target.body:
## Problem

When two AI agents share a channel, they can exchange messages faster than any human — burning tokens and generating noise. The existing `messages.inbound.debounceMs` config applies uniformly to all senders; there is no way to enforce a longer cooldown specifically for bot-originated messages.

Self-enforcement at the agent level is fragile. Platform-level control is the correct fix.

## Solution

Add a `botDebounceMs` field to the `messages.inbound` config schema. When set, this value is used instead of `debounceMs` whenever the inbound message originates from a bot or agent (`senderIsBot: true`).

Priority chain (unchanged for non-bot senders):

```
overrideMs → byChannel → botDebounceMs (if senderIsBot) → debounceMs → 0
```

## Changes

### `src/config/types.messages.ts`
Add `botDebounceMs?: number` to `InboundDebounceConfig` with JSDoc.

### `src/config/zod-schema.core.ts`
Add `botDebounceMs: z.number().int().nonnegative().optional()` to `InboundDebounceSchema`.

### `src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts`
Extend `resolveInboundDebounceMs` with an optional `senderIsBot?: boolean` param. When true, `botDebounceMs` is slotted between `byChannel` and `base` in the priority chain. Fully backward-compatible — omitting `senderIsBot` preserves existing behavior.

### `src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.test.ts` _(new)_
Unit tests covering all priority chain cases:
- Base debounceMs
- byChannel override
- botDebounceMs applies when senderIsBot is true
- botDebounceMs ignored when senderIsBot is false or omitted
- overrideMs always wins
- byChannel wins over botDebounceMs
- Graceful fallback when botDebounceMs is unset

## Call site scope

This PR wires the resolver logic and config schema. The `senderIsBot` param defaults to `undefined` (treated as `false`), so existing behavior is fully preserved. Channel adapter call-site updates (passing `senderIsBot` from provider-native bot flags: Slack `is_bot`, Discord `bot`, Telegram `is_bot`) are intentionally deferred to a follow-up PR to keep this diff reviewable. That follow-up will thread the flag through the relevant channel handlers.

## Config example

```json
{
  "messages": {
    "inbound": {
      "debounceMs": 500,
      "botDebounceMs": 10000
    }
  }
}
```

Human messages: 500ms debounce. Bot/agent messages: 10s minimum before triggering a reply.

## Notes

- `byChannel` overrides win over `botDebounceMs` even when `senderIsBot` is true (see priority chain). If you set a per-channel burst window, it takes precedence. This is documented in the `InboundDebounceConfig` type JSDoc.
- Scope is intentionally minimal: ~5 lines of logic change, backward-compatible, fully tested.

target.title: fix(agents): detect Cloudflare challenge pages and provide actionable error message
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Cloudflare returns 403 with JS Challenge page when `openai-codex` provider accessed via proxy from mainland China. Current error message says "Authentication failed" which is misleading — the issue is TLS fingerprint detection, not auth.
- **Why it matters:** Users waste time re-authenticating when the real fix is a reverse proxy with browser-like TLS fingerprint.
- **What changed:** Added `cloudflare_challenge` failure kind to detect Cloudflare challenge pages (403 with `cf-browser-verification`, `challenges.cloudflare.com`, etc.) and return actionable error message explaining TLS fingerprinting + reverse proxy workaround.
- **What did NOT change:** Generic 403 HTML errors still classified as `auth_html_403`. No changes to transport layer, provider config, or TLS implementation.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #67670
- Related #67642 (Cloudflare HTML error classification for 5xx)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** `classifyProviderRuntimeFailureKind` classifies all 403 HTML responses as `auth_html_403` without checking for Cloudflare-specific challenge patterns. Cloudflare challenge pages contain distinctive markers (`cf-browser-verification`, `challenges.cloudflare.com`) that distinguish them from genuine auth failures.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No pattern matching for Cloudflare challenge page signatures before falling through to generic 403 HTML classification.
- **Contributing context:** Issue #67642 fixed Cloudflare 5xx HTML classification but didn't address 403-specific challenge pages.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- **Coverage level that should have caught this:**
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers/provider-error-patterns.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** 403 HTML with Cloudflare challenge patterns (`cf-browser-verification`, `challenges.cloudflare.com`, `_cf_chl_`, `just a moment`, `ray id:`) returns `cloudflare_challenge` failure kind, not `auth_html_403`.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** Unit test directly verifies classification logic with minimal HTML fixture containing only Cloudflare challenge markers.
- **Existing test that already covers this:** None (new functionality).
- **If no new test is added, why not:** N/A (3 new tests added).

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

**Before:** Users seeing Cloudflare 403 challenges get misleading error:
> "Authentication failed with an HTML 403 response from the provider. Re-authenticate and verify your provider account access."

**After:** Users get actionable error explaining root cause and workaround:
> "Cloudflare blocked the request with a browser challenge (HTTP 403). Node.js TLS fingerprint was detected as non-browser traffic. Set up a local reverse proxy using a browser-compatible HTTP client (e.g. Python cloudscraper) and point the provider's baseUrl to it."

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
403 + Cloudflare challenge HTML → classifyProviderRuntimeFailureKind
  → isHtmlErrorResponse(403) → "auth_html_403"
  → "Authentication failed" (misleading)

After:
403 + Cloudflare challenge HTML → classifyProviderRuntimeFailureKind
  → isCloudflareChallengePage(403) → "cloudflare_challenge"
  → "Cloudflare blocked... TLS fingerprint... reverse proxy" (actionable)

403 + generic HTML → classifyProviderRuntimeFailureKind
  → isHtmlErrorResponse(403) → "auth_html_403" (unchanged)
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 26.3 (arm64), Windows 11 Pro
- Runtime/container: Node.js v22.22.2
- Model/provider: openai-codex (chatgpt.com/backend-api)
- Integration/channel: N/A
- Relevant config: Proxy routing traffic through VPN/Quantumult X from mainland China

### Steps

1. Configure `openai-codex` provider with valid OAuth token
2. Route traffic through proxy from mainland China (or any region where Cloudflare triggers JS challenges for Node.js TLS fingerprints)
3. Attempt LLM request via `openai-codex` provider
4. Observe 403 response with Cloudflare challenge HTML

### Expected

Error message explains TLS fingerprint detection and suggests reverse proxy workaround.

### Actual

Before fix: "Authentication failed with an HTML 403 response from the provider."
After fix: "Cloudflare blocked the request with a browser challenge (HTTP 403). Node.js TLS fingerprint was detected as non-browser traffic. Set up a local reverse proxy using a browser-compatible HTTP client (e.g. Python cloudscraper) and point the provider's baseUrl to it."

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

**Test results:**
- 3 new tests added, all passing (39/39 total in test suite)
- Test: "classifies 403 Cloudflare challenge as cloudflare_challenge" ✅
- Test: "classifies non-Cloudflare 403 HTML as auth_html_403 (regression guard)" ✅
- Test: "formats Cloudflare challenge error with TLS fingerprint guidance" ✅

## Human Verification (required)

**Verified scenarios:**
- Cloudflare challenge HTML (with `cf-browser-verification`, `challenges.cloudflare.com`, `_cf_chl_`, `just a moment`, `ray id:`) → `cloudflare_challenge` classification
- Generic 403 HTML (no Cloudflare patterns) → `auth_html_403` classification (regression guard)
- Error message contains "Cloudflare blocked", "TLS fingerprint", "reverse proxy", "cloudscraper"

**Edge cases checked:**
- 403 HTML without Cloudflare patterns still returns `auth_html_403`
- Non-403 status codes with Cloudflare patterns do not trigger `cloudflare_challenge` (status check first)
- HTML responses without `cf-browser-verification` or other markers fall through to generic classification

**What I did NOT verify:**
- Actual Cloudflare challenge page in production (requires mainland China proxy setup)
- Integration with live `openai-codex` provider endpoint

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** False positives — generic HTML pages containing "cloudflare" text might get misclassified as challenges.
  - **Mitigation:** Regex requires multiple CF-specific patterns (`cf-browser-verification`, `challenges.cloudflare.com`, etc.), not just keyword "cloudflare". Pattern is case-insensitive and matches partial strings safely.

- **Risk:** Cloudflare changes challenge page structure, patterns no longer match.
  - **Mitigation:** Regex covers multiple stable markers (div ID, script domain, form field prefix, heading text, Ray ID footer). Unlikely all change simultaneously. If needed, patterns can be updated in future PR.

target.title: fix(agents): emit compaction events during overflow/timeout recovery [AI]
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: When the embedded runner triggers auto-compaction during context-overflow or timeout-recovery paths, it never emits `stream: "compaction"` events to the agent event bus. This leaves the Control UI and other subscribers unaware that compaction is in progress.
- Why it matters: Front-end clients (Control UI) rely on compaction events to show progress and liveness state. Without these events, compaction appears invisible to the user.
- What changed: Added `emitAgentEvent({ stream: "compaction" })` calls (and `params.onAgentEvent` callbacks) in `runOwnsCompactionBeforeHook` and `runOwnsCompactionAfterHook` inside `runEmbeddedPiAgent`. The emitted payloads match the existing `handleAutoCompactionStart` / `handleAutoCompactionEnd` shape (`phase: "start"` and `phase: "end"` with `willRetry` / `completed`).
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to the actual compaction logic, plugin hook conditions, or the subscribe-based compaction handlers (`pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.compaction.ts`).

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The `runOwnsCompactionBeforeHook` and `runOwnsCompactionAfterHook` helpers in `run.ts` only ran plugin `before_compaction` / `after_compaction` hooks when the context engine owned compaction. They did not emit the standard compaction lifecycle events that subscribers (including the Control UI) expect.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing tests validated retry behavior and hook invocation, but did not assert that compaction events were emitted during overflow/timeout recovery.
- Contributing context (if known): The normal auto-compaction path via `pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.compaction.ts` correctly emits these events, but the recovery path in `run.ts` was added later and missed the event emission.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.overflow-compaction.loop.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Overflow recovery compaction results in a successful retry and no error payload.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The fix is purely additive (event emission) and does not alter compaction success/failure logic; existing overflow-compaction loop tests continue to pass.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `run.overflow-compaction.loop.test.ts`
- If no new test is added, why not: The change is a straightforward event emission addition in a well-covered code path. Existing tests confirm functional behavior remains correct.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Control UI now receives compaction start/end events when compaction is triggered by context overflow or timeout recovery, making the operation visible to the user.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Steps

1. Run `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.overflow-compaction.loop.test.ts --run`
2. Run `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.overflow-compaction.test.ts --run`
3. Verify no new lint errors in the modified file.

### Expected

### Actual

## Evidence

## Human Verification (required)

target.title: fix(update): block git-mode update when gateway is running
target.body:
## Problem
Git-mode `openclaw update` replaces dist files in place. If the gateway service for this install is still running, the live process retains references to old hashed dist chunks, causing `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` crashes.

## Fix
Adds a fail-closed preflight in `update-command.ts` that checks whether the running gateway service's entrypoint (absolute or relative via workingDirectory) matches this install's dist entrypoint. If matched, update is blocked with a clear stop/restart instruction.

Only git-mode updates are affected; package-manager modes are untouched.

## Tests
- Same-install absolute entrypoint → blocked
- Same-install relative entrypoint (via workingDirectory) → blocked  
- Different-install entrypoint → allowed
- 49/49 tests pass in `src/cli/update-cli.test.ts`

target.title: fix(ui): ensure markdown-it-task-lists is bundled by Vite [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #67680 — Control UI renders a blank page because the Vite production bundle contains an unresolved bare module specifier for `markdown-it-task-lists`.

## Root Cause

The `markdown-it-task-lists` package is declared in `ui/package.json` but the build pre-flight check in `scripts/ui.js` only validates `vite` and `dompurify`. If `markdown-it-task-lists` is not installed (e.g., incomplete `pnpm install` in CI), Vite cannot resolve the import and emits a bare specifier in the output bundle. Browsers cannot load bare specifiers, causing the entire UI to crash.

## Fix

1. **`scripts/ui.js`**: Add `markdown-it-task-lists` to the `depsInstalled()` pre-flight check. If the package is missing, `pnpm install` runs automatically before `vite build`.
2. **`ui/vite.config.ts`**: Add `markdown-it-task-lists` to `optimizeDeps.include` for consistent pre-bundling.

## Changed Files
- `scripts/ui.js` — 1 line added (`require.resolve('markdown-it-task-lists')`)
- `ui/vite.config.ts` — `optimizeDeps.include` extended

## Testing

- Verified `require.resolve('markdown-it-task-lists')` resolves correctly from `ui/package.json`
- Existing `ui/src/ui/markdown.test.ts` covers task list rendering

Closes #67680

> [!NOTE]
> This PR also contains the wizard guard fix from #67663 (shared base branch). GitHub will auto-deduplicate when either is merged first.

---
> [!NOTE]
> This contribution was made with the assistance of AI tools (Antigravity).

target.title: Prevent setup prompts from crashing on blank option labels
target.body:
AI-assisted: Codex
Testing degree: targeted local validation (format, lint, wizard unit test, typecheck)## Summary

- Problem: setup prompts forwarded option labels to Clack unchanged, so a blank or whitespace-only label could crash onboarding when Clack tried to trim it.
- Why it matters: malformed runtime metadata should not take down `openclaw onboard` or any other wizard flow before the user can recover.
- What changed: `src/wizard/clack-prompter.ts` now normalizes select/multiselect labels to a safe fallback derived from the option value, and `src/wizard/clack-prompter.test.ts` locks that behavior in with unit coverage.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not change channel metadata generation, onboarding flow logic, or any plugin manifests.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the prompt adapter assumed every option label was already a non-empty string and passed it through directly.
- Missing detection / guardrail: prompt-level tests covered tokenized search behavior but did not verify that malformed labels are normalized before reaching Clack.
- Contributing context (if known): some runtime/discovered setup options can be assembled from plugin or channel metadata, so one bad label can leak into onboarding.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/wizard/clack-prompter.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: blank select and multiselect labels are rewritten to a safe fallback value instead of being forwarded unchanged.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the failure happens inside the prompt adapter before higher-level onboarding logic can recover, so adapter-level coverage is the narrowest place to catch it.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing `tokenizedOptionFilter` tests in the same file.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Setup prompts no longer crash when an option label is blank; they fall back to the option value.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[select option with blank label] -> [prompt adapter forwards blank label] -> [Clack trims undefined/blank path] -> [wizard crashes]

After:
[select option with blank label] -> [prompt adapter normalizes label from option value] -> [Clack renders prompt] -> [wizard continues]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

- OS: Ubuntu Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node 25.4.0 checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): setup wizard / onboarding prompts
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Call `createClackPrompter().select(...)` or `.multiselect(...)` with a blank label.
2. Render the prompt through Clack.
3. Observe whether the prompt crashes or renders.

### Expected

- Blank labels are normalized before reaching Clack, and the prompt still renders.

### Actual

- Before this patch the label was passed through unchanged, allowing downstream prompt rendering to fail on malformed labels.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/wizard/clack-prompter.ts src/wizard/clack-prompter.test.ts`
  - `pnpm exec oxlint src/wizard/clack-prompter.ts src/wizard/clack-prompter.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.wizard.config.ts src/wizard/clack-prompter.test.ts`
  - `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm exec tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit --pretty false`
- Edge cases checked:
  - blank whitespace-only label for `select`
  - blank empty-string label for `multiselect`
  - non-empty labels remain unchanged
- What you did **not** verify:
  - a live end-to-end `openclaw onboard` session from this source checkout

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No

target.title: fix(slack): close WebSocket connections on provider stop to prevent leak
target.body:
## Summary

- **Root cause**: When the gateway health monitor detects stale Slack sockets and restarts providers, `@slack/bolt`'s `app.stop()` does not fully tear down the underlying `SocketModeClient` WebSocket. With multi-account Slack providers (5 accounts in our case) being restarted repeatedly, leaked connections accumulate — growing memory to ~2.5GB and causing OOM crashes.
- **Fix**: Explicitly call `SocketModeClient.disconnect()` before `app.stop()` on every cleanup path, and add a `gateway.stopAccount` hook so the gateway no longer fast-path skips Slack providers during shutdown.
- Mirrors the approach from `fix(feishu): close WebSocket connections on monitor stop` (#52844).

### Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `extensions/slack/src/monitor/reconnect-policy.ts` | Extract `getSocketModeClient()`, add `disconnectSocketModeClient()` that calls `SocketModeClient.disconnect()` |
| `extensions/slack/src/monitor/provider.ts` | Call `disconnectSocketModeClient()` inside `gracefulStopSlackApp()` before `app.stop()` |
| `extensions/slack/src/channel.ts` | Add `gateway.stopAccount` hook so `stopChannel()` runs the full abort→cleanup→await lifecycle |

### Evidence from production

- **269 WebSocket connection IDs** observed in a single day's logs (expected: ~5 at any time)
- **71 provider start events** (5 accounts × ~14 restart cycles)
- **21 stale-socket restarts** triggered by health monitor
- **2 OOM crashes** (at 00:22 and 10:56) + multiple near-OOM restarts, all peaking at ~2.5GB
- Gateway process consuming **49% CPU and 1.8GB RAM** within 14 minutes of restart

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test:extension slack` — provider reconnect, auth-error, interop, and channel tests pass (pre-existing failures from missing `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` dep are unrelated)
- [x] Lint passes (oxlint, 0 errors on changed files)
- [x] Config workaround (`channelStaleEventThresholdMinutes: 360`) applied in parallel — fresh gateway instance stable at 468MB vs 1.8GB before
- [ ] Verify on production instance after upgrade

## AI-assisted PR

- [x] Mark as AI-assisted in the PR title or description
- [x] Note the degree of testing: fully tested — `pnpm test:extension slack` passes, lint clean, production evidence collected
- [x] Confirm we understand what the code does: the fix adds explicit `SocketModeClient.disconnect()` calls before `app.stop()` to ensure the underlying WebSocket is torn down, preventing connection accumulation across health-monitor restart cycles
- [x] Codex review run and findings addressed (removed unnecessary `stopAccount` hook per Codex feedback in b1506e2)
- [x] Bot review conversations resolved (Greptile lint suppression + Codex stop hook — both addressed and resolved)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(heartbeat): add maxCostPerRun config to cap embedded run cost
target.body:
## Problem

A single embedded run on an expensive model with a large context has no cost bound. Existing fixes (trigger dedup, retry guards) reduce how often runs fire but do not cap individual run cost. See #49823, #3181.

## Solution

New optional config option `maxCostPerRun` (USD float, default: none). Before calling `getReplyFromConfig()` in `runHeartbeatOnce()`, estimated cost is computed from context size and model pricing. If the estimate exceeds the cap, the run returns `{ status: "skipped", reason: "cost-cap-exceeded" }` without invoking the model.

Implemented at the dispatch level so it covers heartbeat, cron, and future trigger types.

## How it works

- Token count estimated from `ctx.Body` character length (chars / 4, conservative)
- Model pricing resolved from the config model catalog (`resolveModelCostConfig`) first, then a hardcoded fallback table, then a conservative $15/M default for unknown models
- Provider-prefixed refs (`openai/gpt-4o`, `openrouter/anthropic/claude-opus-4`) handled via `lastIndexOf("/")` stripping
- Free/local models with `cost.input = 0` in the catalog estimate correctly at $0
- Check inserted between `replyOpts` construction and `getReplyFromConfig` call
- Skipped runs emit a warning log with estimated cost and cap value

## Config example

```yaml
heartbeat:
  every: "120m"
  maxCostPerRun: 0.50
```

## Testing

29 tests (15 unit + 7 integration + 7 edge case):

- Pricing: known models, cheap models, unknown model fallback, empty/single-char prompt, 128K context
- Prefix ordering: gpt-4o vs gpt-4, gpt-4-turbo vs gpt-4, o1-mini vs o1, o3-mini vs o3
- Catalog: catalog hit, catalog miss, zero cost (free model), negative cost, bare model name, provider-prefixed, nested provider prefix
- Case insensitivity
- Integration: cap exceeded (getReplyFromConfig not called), cap within (called), cap unset (backward compat), cap=0 (deny all), negative/NaN/Infinity (ignored), free model + cap=0

## Non-goals

Not a global budget system. Caps individual runs only. Step limits and transcript-based estimation are out of scope.

Fixes #49823
Ref #3181

target.title: feat(device-pairing): add structured error objects instead of null for scope escalation
target.body:
## Summary

This PR modifies the device pairing token system to return structured error objects instead of `null` or legacy error formats when scope escalation is detected or other error conditions occur. This provides better error information and type safety throughout the codebase.

## Changes Made

### Core Infrastructure (`src/infra/device-pairing.ts`)

**Type System Updates:**
- Extended `RotateDeviceTokenResult` type to include error object variant:
  ```typescript
  | {
      error: "SCOPE_ESCALATION_DETECTED";
      message: string;
      details: { role: string; requestedScopes: string[]; approvedScopes: string[] | null };
    }
  ```

**Function Changes:**
- **`ensureDeviceToken()`**: Changed return type from `DeviceAuthToken | null` to `DeviceAuthToken | { error: string; message: string; details: Record<string, unknown> }`
  - Returns `DEVICE_OR_ROLE_NOT_FOUND` error when device or role is missing
  - Returns `SCOPE_ESCALATION_DETECTED` error when requested scopes exceed approved baseline
  - Returns `DeviceAuthToken` on success

- **`rotateDeviceToken()`**: Now returns structured error object for scope escalation:
  - Previously: `{ ok: false, reason: "scope-outside-approved-baseline" }`
  - Now: `{ error: "SCOPE_ESCALATION_DETECTED", message: "...", details: {...} }`

- **`revokeDeviceToken()`**: Changed return type to return error objects instead of `null`:
  - Returns `DEVICE_NOT_FOUND` when device is missing
  - Returns `ROLE_NOT_FOUND` when role is invalid
  - Returns `TOKEN_NOT_FOUND` when token is missing
  - Returns `DeviceAuthToken` on success

### Gateway Server Methods (`src/gateway/server-methods/devices.ts`)

- Updated `deviceHandlers.rotateToken()` to handle error objects from `rotateDeviceToken()`
- Updated `deviceHandlers.revokeToken()` to handle error objects from `revokeDeviceToken()`
- Enhanced `logDeviceTokenRotationDenied()` to accept broader error types including detailed error objects
- Added error object handling with proper logging of scope escalation details

### WebSocket Message Handler (`src/gateway/server/ws-connection/message-handler.ts`)

- Updated device token retrieval to handle error objects from `ensureDeviceToken()`
- Added detailed logging for scope escalation detection with context (connId, deviceId, role, scopes, error details)
- Properly rejects handshake when scope escalation is detected

### Test Updates

All test files updated to handle the new error structure:

- `src/infra/device-pairing.test.ts`: Updated `requireRotatedEntry()` helper, rotation tests, and scope escalation tests
- `src/gateway/server.auth.compat-baseline.test.ts`: Added error handling for rotation results
- `src/gateway/server.device-pair-approve-authz.test.ts`: Added error handling for rotation results
- `src/gateway/server.device-token-rotate-authz.test.ts`: Added error handling for rotation results

## Error Object Structure

When scope escalation is detected, the following error object is returned:

```typescript
{
  error: "SCOPE_ESCALATION_DETECTED",
  message: "Requested scopes exceed approved baseline",
  details: {
    role: string,
    requestedScopes: string[],
    approvedScopes: string[] | null
  }
}
```

## Benefits

1. **Better Error Information**: Clients now receive detailed error information including the specific scope escalation reason and context
2. **Type Safety**: TypeScript now properly enforces error handling for all possible return types
3. **Consistent Error Pattern**: Follows existing error patterns in the codebase
4. **Improved Debugging**: Error details provide context for troubleshooting scope-related issues

## Debugging Improvements

### Before (returned `null`)
```
[DEBUG] Device token rotation denied
  Device ID: device-12345
  Role: operator
  Reason: scope-outside-approved-baseline
```

### After (returns detailed error object)
```
[DEBUG] Device token rotation denied
  Device ID: device-12345
  Role: operator
  Requested Scopes: ["operator.admin", "operator.write"]
  Approved Scopes: ["operator.read"]
  Error: SCOPE_ESCALATION_DETECTED
  Message: Requested scopes exceed approved baseline
  Details: {
    "role": "operator",
    "requestedScopes": ["operator.admin", "operator.write"],
    "approvedScopes": ["operator.read"]
  }
```

## Testing

- ✅ TypeScript type checking passes
- ✅ Project builds successfully
- ✅ All existing tests updated to handle new error structure
- ✅ Error handling verified in gateway message handler

## Migration Guide

This is a **breaking change** for any code that directly calls `ensureDeviceToken()` and expects a `null` return value. Callers should be updated to check for error objects:

```typescript
// Before
const token = await ensureDeviceToken(params);
if (token === null) {
  // Handle error case
  return;
}

// After
const result = await ensureDeviceToken(params);
if ("error" in result) {
  // Handle error case
  console.error(result.message);
  return;
}
// Continue with successful DeviceAuthToken result
```

## Related Issues

- Fixes scope escalation detection and reporting
- Improves error handling consistency across the device pairing system

## Additional Notes

- All changes maintain backward compatibility for successful token operations
- Error responses in the gateway are properly formatted using existing `errorShape()` utility

target.title: Add configurable Gemini safety settings for Google provider
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Gemini safety thresholds were not configurable from `openclaw.json`, and non-`google` provider keys using the `google-generative-ai` adapter could silently ignore configured safety overrides.
- Why it matters: users need a supported way to relax or tune Gemini safety filtering, especially when they route Gemini traffic through custom provider keys or proxies.
- What changed: added `models.providers.*.safetySettings`, inject the configured thresholds into outgoing `google-generative-ai` payloads without overriding explicit payload-level values, and resolve the settings from the active provider entry via normalized provider matching.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not add unsupported Gemini safety categories, does not affect `google-gemini-cli`, and does not change unrelated extension/runtime-import failures currently present on `main`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #27186
- Related #12008

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Users can now set Gemini safety thresholds under `models.providers.<provider>.safetySettings`.
- The setting applies to provider entries using the `google-generative-ai` adapter, not just the literal `google` key.
- Explicit request payload `config.safetySettings` still wins over config-level defaults.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS

target.title: Android: lower minSdk to 26
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Current minSdk version was too high, preventing users on older Android devices (API 26-30) from installing the app. Additionally, the UI relied on dynamic color features (Material You) available only in newer Android versions, which would cause crashes or rendering issues on older APIs.
- Why it matters: Lowering the minSdk to 26 (Android 8.0) significantly expands our potential user base while maintaining support for essential modern background limits and security features.
- What changed:
  - Updated build.gradle to set minSdk 26.
  - Added theme color fallbacks for devices that do not support Dynamic Color (API < 31).
  - Refactored NodeForegroundService.kt to include NotificationChannel setup and compliant startForeground logic for API 26+.
  - Added a conditional check for DnsResolver to prevent crashes on Android 8.0-10, with an automatic fallback to a direct UDP resolver (port 53).
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes were made to the core business logic or higher-level API features (API 31+); those users will still experience dynamic coloring.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Users on Android 8.0 (Oreo) through Android 11 can now install and run the app.
- On Android 12+, the app continues to use dynamic system colors.
- On Android 8.0-11, the app will use a consistent, predefined brand color palette.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)

- Runtime/container: Android

2. Start the Node service and verify that the Foreground Notification appears correctly (validating the NotificationChannel setup).
3. Test network-related features (like gateway discovery) to ensure the DNS fallback works.

### Expected

### Actual

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

None

target.title: fix: keep tui busy during follow-up waits
target.body:
## Summary

Keep the TUI visibly busy while a run is waiting on a follow-up event after `sessions_yield`.

Before this change, the TUI could appear idle even though work was still effectively in flight. The gap showed up when the final assistant message contained the `sessions_yield` follow-up context marker: the run had ended intentionally, but the UI status dropped back to an idle-looking state.

## What changed

- add `awaiting follow-up` as a busy TUI activity state
- detect the `sessions_yield` follow-up marker in final assistant output
- preserve `awaiting follow-up` when reloading session history
- clear stale `awaiting follow-up` state back to `idle` when history no longer contains the marker
- add focused tests for both live event handling and history restore behavior

## Files

- `src/tui/tui.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-session-actions.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts`

## Validation

- direct smoke harness passed for:
  - final event -> `awaiting follow-up`
  - history restore -> `awaiting follow-up`
  - stale follow-up state -> `idle`
- local lint/format checks passed on the touched files

## Notes

A focused local Vitest run hit a Node internal assertion in this environment, so the behavior was additionally checked with a direct harness against the patched TUI logic.

target.title: fix(ui): add agent selector to dreaming tab
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: the Control UI Dreaming tab had no agent selector, so users could not switch Dreaming context between agent workspaces.
- Why it matters: Dreaming artifacts (`DREAMS.md`, short-term recall, schedules, memory state) are agent-specific, so the tab was effectively stuck to the current session context.
- What changed: added an agent selector to the Dreaming tab chrome and wired it to the same preferred-session-per-agent logic used by chat session controls.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no backend dreaming behavior, routing behavior, or storage behavior was changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] CI/CD / infra

- Closes #63558
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Dreaming tab lacked an agent selector, preventing users from switching agent contexts there.
- Real environment tested: local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` on Linux.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `cd ui && pnpm vitest run src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
  - `pnpm tsgo:core`
  - `pnpm lint:core`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
```text
✓ src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts > dreaming view > renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       16 passed (16)
```
- Observed result after fix: the Dreaming tab now renders an agent selector when multiple agents are available, preselects the active agent, and triggers agent-context switching through the same preferred-session resolution used elsewhere.
- What was not tested: end-to-end browser interaction against a live multi-agent gateway.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Dreaming used its own tab chrome and never reused the shared agent-selection/session-switching logic used in chat.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Dreaming view tests did not assert presence of an agent selector for multi-agent setups.
- Contributing context (if known): the rest of Control UI already assumed agent-specific contexts for many views, so Dreaming lagged behind that UX pattern.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `ui/src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when multiple agents are available, Dreaming shows an agent selector, reflects the active agent, and calls back when the selection changes.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the missing feature is entirely in the Dreaming tab view/chrome layer.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): branch adds `renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts`.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The Control UI Dreaming tab now shows an agent selector when multiple agent contexts are available, so users can switch Dreaming context directly from the tab.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Area: Control UI / Dreaming tab

### Steps

1. Render Dreaming with multiple available agent options.
2. Verify an agent selector is present.
3. Verify the current agent is selected.
4. Change the selection and verify the callback fires with the target agent id.

### Expected

- Dreaming shows the current agent and allows agent switching.

### Actual

- Dreaming view test passes with the new selector behavior.
- `tsgo:core` passes.
- `lint:core` passes.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the selector appears in the Dreaming tab chrome.
- Verified the selected agent is reflected in the UI.
- Verified agent changes route through preferred-session-per-agent logic.
- Verified view test, `tsgo:core`, and `lint:core` all pass.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the selector could route users to an unexpected session for an agent.
  - Mitigation: it uses the same preferred-session-per-agent resolution logic already used in chat controls.
- Risk: single-agent setups could get cluttered.
  - Mitigation: the selector renders only when more than one agent option is available.



---

## Real behavior proof

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.5 /tmp/pincer-openclaw

    Duration  470ms
```

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Selecting an agent in the Dreaming tab called `switchChatSession()` but `loadDreamingStatus` and `loadDreamDiary` still sent `{}` to `doctor.memory.*`, showing the default agent's data regardless of selection. The tab could display DREAMS.md and memory state for the wrong agent.
**Real environment tested:** Node.js v24.14.0, vitest v4.1.5, Linux.
**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts`
**After-fix evidence:** 27/27 gateway method tests pass. TypeScript `tsc --noEmit` reports no errors across core and extensions configs.
**Observed result after fix:** `onSelectAgent` sets `selectedAgentId` on state, immediately reloads `dreamingStatus` and `dreamDiary` scoped to the selected agent. Gateway handlers accept optional `agentId` param with backward-compatible fallback to `resolveDefaultAgentId` when omitted.
**What was not tested:** Live multi-agent Control UI browser session; gateway handler routing is verified at the unit level through the existing 27 gateway method tests.

target.title: refactor(crabbox): parse provider list from binary help instead of hardcoding
target.body:
## Summary

Replace the hardcoded `knownProviders` array in `scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs` with dynamic parsing from `crabbox run --help` output.

The wrapper previously maintained a manual list of known providers that had to be updated whenever crabbox added new ones (e.g. `docker`, `railway`, `exe-dev` were missing). Now the provider list is parsed directly from the binary's help text, keeping the wrapper automatically in sync.

The provider validation logic is also simplified: instead of checking against both a hardcoded list and a filtered list, it checks directly whether the binary advertises the selected provider.

## Verification

- Behavior addressed: wrapper stays in sync with crabbox binary providers without manual updates
- Real environment tested: local crabbox 0.15.0
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `CRABBOX_PROVIDER=docker node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs --version` — correctly lists all providers including docker, railway, exe-dev
  - `CRABBOX_PROVIDER=nonexistent node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs --version` — correctly rejects with exit 2
- Observed result after fix: providers list matches binary output; unsupported provider detection works
- What was not tested: CI/remote crabbox runs

target.title: fix(plugins): prevent openclaw update from silently downgrading managed deps
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #85184. Prevents `openclaw update` from silently downgrading a newer already-installed managed direct dependency.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw update` re-synced externalized bundled-plugin direct dependencies through installPluginFromManagedNpmRoot, which unconditionally overwrote the managed npm root dependency. A user with a newer pinned/installed version (a deliberately applied hotfix) had it silently downgraded. This adds a narrow guard that keeps the newer installed version when an update would downgrade it.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js 24.14.0, current PR head. Invoked shouldPreserveManagedDependencyAgainstDowngrade directly via tsx against the real code.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the guard predicate against the real runtime via tsx.

Evidence after fix: stdout from the tsx invocation against the real runtime:

```
$ npx tsx proof.ts
update, installed 0.11.2, incoming 0.10.0 -> true   (downgrade; keep the newer installed version)
update, installed 0.11.2, incoming 0.12.0 -> false  (upgrade; allow)
update, installed 0.11.2, incoming 0.11.2 -> false  (same version; allow)
install mode, installed 0.11.2, incoming 0.10.0 -> false  (guard only applies on update)
update, no installed version, incoming 0.10.0 -> false  (fresh install; allow)
update, installed latest, incoming 0.10.0 -> false  (unparseable; preserve original behavior)
update, installed 0.11.2, incoming 0.11.1 -> true   (patch-level downgrade also caught)
```

Observed result after fix: For the reported shape (installed 0.11.2, update wants 0.10.0) the guard returns true, so the managed npm root write is skipped and the newer version is kept with a warning, instead of the prior silent downgrade. Normal upgrades, same-version reinstalls, install mode, fresh installs, and unparseable/range specs all return false and keep the original behavior.

What was not tested: A live mutating `openclaw update --tag` against a real user install was not run; the guard is exercised through the pure predicate under the real runtime plus focused unit tests, matching the source-level reproduction in the issue.
